The item may not qualify for capitalisation, but it may still need a clear movement history and custodian. That is why the register should serve more than one purpose. It is not only a financial schedule. It is also a stewardship tool that helps prevent loss, misuse, and confusion. A low-value item can still become a significant issue if it is important to service delivery or difficult to replace quickly.

Tracking also helps reveal patterns. If the same type of non-capital item repeatedly disappears, the organisation may have a custody problem that is invisible in the expense ledger alone.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IPSASB — Official site
Q: How should donated assets and in-kind items be treated?
A: Show answer
Donated assets should be accepted formally, described properly, tagged if needed, and recorded in the asset register. The fact that no cash was paid does not remove the need for control. If the organisation controls the item and benefits from it, the item still has to be managed through the asset lifecycle. The file should show who donated it, what was received, when it arrived, what condition it was in, and whether there were any restrictions. That creates a complete history from the beginning.

If the donated item is capitalised, the valuation basis should be supportable and consistent with the applicable accounting framework. If it is not capitalised, it may still need to be recorded as a controlled item for custody and operational tracking. In either case, the item should not vanish into informal use. Donation is only the source of the item, not the end of the control process. Once it is received, it should be treated like any other asset under management. The absence of an invoice does not mean the absence of responsibility.

Donated assets should also be checked for donor restrictions. Some gifts are simple, while others carry conditions about use, transfer, or disposal. Those conditions should be visible in the file so they are not forgotten later.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- IPSASB — Official site
Q: How should restricted funds affect asset accounting?
A: Show answer
Restricted funds matter because the donor or grant agreement may impose conditions on ownership, use, movement, or disposal. The organisation should not assume an asset is unrestricted simply because it is useful operationally. The terms of the funding may require the asset to stay within a project, remain within a certain location, or be returned or approved before disposal. If those conditions are ignored, the asset may be misclassified from the start. That can create reporting and compliance problems later.

The file should retain the document that creates the restriction. That way, future staff can see why the asset was treated the way it was treated. Restrictions should remain visible throughout the asset’s life, not just at acquisition. If a sale, transfer, or write-off is later considered, the file should make the restriction easy to find. Good documentation prevents accidental breaches of donor terms. It also protects the organisation if questions arise long after the original grant team has moved on.

Restricted conditions may also affect accounting disclosures, especially where ownership or disposal rights are limited. The treatment should follow the actual agreement, not a simplified assumption.

Official links:
- IPSASB — Official site
- GAO Green Book
Q: How should transfer and ownership conditions be handled for donor-funded assets?
A: Show answer
The organisation should first determine whether it owns the asset, merely controls it, or is holding it on behalf of another party. That distinction matters because ownership affects capitalisation, disclosure, transfer rights, and disposal authority. A donor-funded item may still belong to the organisation, but the donor terms could restrict how it is used. In other cases, the organisation may only be the custodian. Those differences should be visible in the register and in the supporting file. Clear ownership treatment prevents later disputes.

If the legal or contractual condition is stricter than internal policy, the stricter valid condition should govern. Staff should not simplify the treatment because the file is inconvenient. The asset record should follow the substance of the arrangement. That is the only way to maintain truthful reporting and proper control. The more clearly the ownership basis is recorded, the easier it is to manage the asset later when circumstances change. Ownership clarity also matters if the item is transferred to another site, returned to the donor, or replaced through another grant.

The register should make ownership status obvious at a glance so staff do not confuse donor-funded with donor-owned or organisation-owned with controlled only.

Official links:
- IPSASB — Official site
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
Q: Why is physical verification at year-end so important?
A: Show answer
Year-end verification tests whether the register still matches the real world at the reporting date. It is the strongest check on whether assets actually exist, are in the expected location, and remain serviceable. A register can look accurate on paper while still containing missing, transferred, damaged, or obsolete items. Physical verification exposes those differences and allows corrections before the financial statements are finalised. Without a count, the organisation is largely trusting the paperwork rather than the asset itself.

The count should be planned, documented, and reviewed. Items should be checked against tags, serial numbers, and recorded locations where possible. Exceptions should be investigated, not ignored. The count result should feed directly into adjustments, write-offs, or transfer updates where required. The value of the count lies not in the act of counting alone, but in the control action that follows it. A count that produces no action does not improve the system much. A count that leads to correction and follow-up strengthens the entire control environment.

Physical verification also helps identify patterns such as repeated missing items in one office, repeated location errors, or recurring custodian changes that were never recorded properly.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
Q: What should happen when an asset is lost, stolen, damaged, or written off?
A: Show answer
The event should be reported immediately and documented clearly. The report should include the asset number, description, last known location, custodian, date last seen, and the circumstances of the incident. If the item is damaged, the file should show whether repair is possible and whether repair is economical. If the item is stolen or lost, the organisation should review whether the matter requires security follow-up, insurance action, or management attention. Write-off should happen only after the facts are reviewed and the item is no longer recoverable or serviceable.

Loss and write-off should not become informal shortcuts for cleaning up the register. A weak write-off process can hide repeated control failures. The organisation should use the incident as a learning point. If the same type of loss keeps happening in the same place, that is a sign that the underlying control environment needs strengthening. Write-off closes the accounting record, but not the control responsibility. The goal is not only to remove the asset from the books, but also to understand what failed so the same event does not keep happening.

Good reporting also creates a clear audit trail. That helps if the event must later be explained to donors, auditors, insurers, or senior management.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- COSO — Official site
Q: Why is disposal different from ordinary movement or transfer?
A: Show answer
Disposal means the asset leaves active organisational control. Movement or transfer means the item is still within the organisation’s control chain, just in a different place or with a different custodian. Disposal is therefore a final event and must be supported by evidence, approval, and a register update. The organisation should know whether the item was sold, scrapped, donated, destroyed, returned, or written off. Each method has different accounting and control consequences. Confusing disposal with transfer is one of the common causes of register errors.

Before disposal, management should consider whether the item still has useful life or residual value. A sale may recover value, while scrap may be appropriate if the item is no longer usable. Whatever the method, the register should reflect the outcome clearly. The asset should not remain on the books after it has left control. A clean disposal record protects the organisation from overstated assets and incomplete histories. It also protects future staff who need to understand why the asset disappeared and when that happened.

The disposal file should also show that any donor or legal restriction was respected before the item left control. That avoids later disputes about whether the item was disposed of properly.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- GAO Green Book
Q: What should happen when a fixed asset is sold?
A: Show answer
A sale should be supported by the buyer identity, the asset details, the date, the approved sale amount, and evidence that the item actually left control. For capital assets, the original cost and accumulated depreciation should be removed from the books, and any gain or loss should be recognised according to policy. The proceeds should be recorded and handled according to the organisation’s financial rules. A sale without proper documentation can create confusion about whether the item was really disposed of and whether the cash was received correctly. The sale file should therefore close both the physical and accounting loops.

The sale process should also protect the organisation from undervaluation and conflict of interest. A sold asset should not simply disappear into informal arrangements. If there is any concern about value, approval, or related-party risk, the process should be more carefully documented. The more transparent the sale, the easier it is to defend later. It should also be clear whether sale proceeds are treated as general income, replacement reserve, or another authorised category under policy.

Sale records should remain with the archive because later questions often arise about disposal timing, proceeds, and the rationale for the sale method. Good documentation is the safeguard.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- GAO Green Book
Q: Why should asset records be kept even after disposal?
A: Show answer
Because disposal is not the same as disappearance. The organisation may still need the history of the item later for audit, donor review, internal investigation, or comparison against prior periods. The disposal record explains what happened, when it happened, who approved it, and what the outcome was. That historical trail can be just as important as the active register. Without it, the organisation may be unable to explain an old decision or resolve a later question about a missing balance.

Records should therefore be archived according to retention requirements. The archive should preserve the full story from acquisition to final removal. That includes approvals, invoices, movement forms, count results, disposal documents, and any valuation support. The organisation’s memory should not depend only on the people who were present at the time. A good archive makes the asset history retrievable long after the asset itself is gone. That matters when staff change, projects close, or donors request evidence well after the asset has been disposed of.

Keeping the history also helps if the organisation repeats the same type of purchase later. Past disposal records can show what wore out quickly, what sold well, and what created maintenance problems.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
Q: Why is segregation of duties important in fixed asset control?
A: Show answer
Segregation of duties reduces the risk that one person can request, approve, receive, record, and dispose of an asset without independent review. If all those steps can be completed by the same person, it becomes much easier to hide mistakes or improper actions. A well-designed process separates the key stages as far as staffing allows. Where full separation is not possible, compensating controls such as review, approval, or spot checks should be added. The purpose is not bureaucracy. It is risk reduction.

Even in a small office, the principle still matters. The organisation should identify where duties overlap and then add review at the most sensitive points, especially acquisition, disposal, and register update. Separation is most valuable where assets can be moved, sold, or written off. That is where misuse is easiest to hide if no one else checks the work. Segregation also improves confidence because the people involved are less likely to be blamed for problems they did not create.

When perfect separation is impossible, the organisation should document the compensating control rather than pretending the risk does not exist. Honest control design is better than theoretical control design.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- COSO — Official site
Q: Why should fixed asset control be risk-based rather than identical for every item?
A: Show answer
Because not every asset creates the same exposure. A large vehicle, a portable laptop, a medical device, or a generator may need stronger custody and approval controls than a fixed low-risk item that is difficult to move. A risk-based framework allows the organisation to spend control effort where it matters most. That makes the system realistic and more likely to be followed. It also prevents unnecessary administration around low-risk items. The aim is to protect value without making the process so heavy that staff stop using it properly.

Risk-based control means the safeguards should match the likelihood and consequence of loss. High-risk items may need tagging, restricted access, regular verification, and multi-level approval for movement. Lower-risk items may need only lighter controls. The organisation should periodically reassess risk because an item that was low-risk last year may become more sensitive later. The control design should evolve with the environment, not remain fixed forever. A good control system is not rigid; it responds to changing operational reality.

Risk-based design also helps management allocate attention. The most important assets get the strongest scrutiny, while lower-risk items remain manageable.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- COSO — Official site
Q: What should a practical acquisition checklist include?
A: Show answer
The checklist should confirm budget or approval, procurement compliance, receipt, condition, tagging, registration, and supporting documents such as invoices and installation costs. It should also ask whether the item is capital or non-capital, whether donor conditions apply, and whether a custodian has been assigned. The purpose is to stop omissions before the asset enters the system. If an item is physically received but not properly documented, control has already weakened. A checklist closes that gap early.

Checklists are useful because they standardise routine work and reduce memory errors. In a busy office, people may forget a step simply because the transaction volume is high. A checklist prevents that by turning the process into a repeatable routine. It also helps supervisors see whether the same procedure is being followed across different locations. That consistency is one of the biggest strengths of a good asset system. It creates a common standard across offices and projects instead of leaving each team to improvise.

A practical checklist should be short enough to use and specific enough to catch common failure points. If it is too long, staff may ignore it. If it is too vague, it will not prevent errors.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- GAO Green Book
Q: What should happen when assets are received as a donation or in-kind contribution?
A: Show answer
The organisation should formally accept the item, document its condition, note any restrictions, assign a custodian, and record it in the register if it is controlled or capitalisable. The absence of a supplier invoice does not remove the need for evidence. Donation records should show the donor, the date received, the item description, and any conditions attached to its use or disposal. If the item is capitalised, the valuation basis should be supportable. If it is not capitalised, it may still need to be recorded as a controlled item. Either way, the item should not be left outside the control system simply because no cash was paid.

Donated assets often create confusion later if the original file is weak. The organisation should therefore treat the donation record as the starting point of the asset lifecycle. That record becomes important again if the item is transferred, sold, written off, or reviewed for donor compliance. A clear donation trail protects the organisation from uncertainty later. It also helps confirm whether the item was a simple gift, a restricted gift, or a condition-based transfer that still carries obligations.

Acceptance should also be deliberate. The organisation should avoid casually taking items that create maintenance, storage, or compliance burdens without checking whether it can actually manage them properly.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- IPSASB — Official site
Q: How should a good asset register support audit and compliance?
A: Show answer
It should allow an auditor or reviewer to trace each item from purchase or donation to disposal without guessing. That means there must be source documents, clear approvals, exact descriptions, location data, custodian details, and disposal evidence where relevant. The register should not simply say an item exists. It should explain why the item is there and how it has been treated over time. That is the difference between a list and an audit-ready control record.

Compliance also means following the strictest valid rule among law, donor conditions, and internal policy. Staff should not use convenience as a reason to weaken control. If the organisation can explain every major asset decision clearly, audit becomes easier and management confidence improves. If it cannot explain the decisions, the control environment needs work. Good audit support is built by good recordkeeping throughout the year, not by emergency cleanup at the end. The register should therefore be maintained as if it may be reviewed at any time, because in practice it often is.

Audit-ready records also reduce the burden on finance staff because questions can be answered quickly from evidence rather than reconstructed from memory.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
Q: Why is a year-end physical count not enough on its own?
A: Show answer
A year-end count is important, but it is only one control point. If the register has not been maintained during the year, a single count may uncover many differences too late to fix cleanly. Monthly reconciliation and transfer control are needed so the year-end count confirms an already disciplined system rather than rescuing a neglected one. The count should therefore be the final verification step, not the only verification step. Control works best when routine updates happen continuously and the year-end count merely confirms them.

A count should also lead to action. If the organisation finds missing assets, wrong locations, or broken items, those differences should be investigated and recorded. The value of the count lies in what happens after it. A count that is not followed by adjustments or control improvement is just a ritual. The system becomes stronger when the count is used to correct the record and improve future behaviour. It is the combination of counting, investigating, and updating that creates real control.

For that reason, the year-end count should be coordinated with the monthly process so that the organisation does not create two separate versions of the truth.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- COSO — Official site
Q: What is the most important final lesson from the fixed assets chapter?
A: Show answer
The most important lesson is that fixed assets must be managed as a lifecycle, not as isolated transactions. The organisation should plan the purchase, classify it properly, capitalise or expense it correctly, tag it, assign custody, depreciate it, reconcile it, count it physically, and dispose of it with evidence. Every stage depends on the stage before it. If acquisition is weak, everything after that becomes harder. If transfer and disposal are weak, the register stops reflecting reality. A strong asset system is one where the physical item, the register, and the supporting documents all tell the same story.

That is what good stewardship looks like. It is not just about avoiding audit findings. It is about knowing what the organisation has, protecting it while it is in service, and removing it properly when it is no longer needed. When the record and the reality match, management can trust the system. That trust is the real value of fixed asset control. It improves reporting, supports planning, strengthens accountability, and makes the organisation more credible to donors, auditors, and management alike.

The chapter therefore ends with one simple standard: if the item exists, it should be traceable; if it is controlled, it should be recorded; and if it leaves service, it should leave a documented trail behind it.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- GAO Green Book
- IPSASB — Official site
Q: When should an asset be treated as a work in progress instead of a completed fixed asset?
A: Show answer
An item should stay in work in progress, or capital work in progress, when it is still being built, installed, tested, or otherwise prepared and is not yet ready for its intended use. The accounting point is simple: depreciation should not start until the asset is available for use. If a building is still under construction, or equipment is still being configured, the cost belongs in a construction-in-progress category rather than in the final asset class. That keeps depreciation from starting too early and prevents the asset register from showing a finished item before it actually exists in usable form.

Work in progress also needs control. Staff should track progress payments, contractor certificates, variation orders, retention, and expected completion dates. A project that is almost complete is not the same as a usable asset, and the register should show that distinction clearly. If the item is moved into fixed assets too early, depreciation may begin while the asset is still not contributing to operations. If it is moved too late, the books may understate completed assets. A disciplined cut-off is therefore necessary at each reporting date.

Project teams and finance teams should agree on the handover point. The asset should only be transferred from work in progress to fixed assets when it has been accepted and is ready for use under the organisation’s policy.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- IAS 23 Borrowing Costs — IFRS Foundation
Q: When do borrowing costs belong in the cost of a fixed asset?
A: Show answer
Borrowing costs may be capitalised only when the applicable accounting framework and policy allow it, and only for qualifying assets that take a substantial period of time to get ready for use. The reason is that some assets are built or developed over time, and the financing cost is part of bringing them to the condition required for use. If the project is routine, small, or quickly completed, borrowing costs are usually expensed instead. The key is not the existence of a loan, but whether the asset qualifies under the relevant rule set.

This area should be treated carefully because it affects both asset cost and financing expense. The file should clearly show the dates, rates, capitalisation period, and calculation method used. If the asset is under construction for a long period, the capitalised interest can be material. If the period is short or the asset does not meet the qualifying criteria, capitalisation should not be forced. The policy should therefore define exactly when borrowing costs start and stop, and who approves the calculation.

Good support is essential. The calculation should be transparent enough that another person can follow it later without guesswork. If the organisation uses projects financed by loans or grant-linked borrowing, the distinction should be documented from the start rather than argued after completion.

Official links:
- IAS 23 Borrowing Costs — IFRS Foundation
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
Q: Why should useful life and residual value be reviewed instead of copied forever?
A: Show answer
Useful life and residual value are estimates, not permanent truths. An asset may last longer or shorter than expected depending on usage, environment, maintenance, and technology changes. If the organisation copies the original estimate year after year without review, depreciation can become unrealistic. The purpose of the estimate is to reflect the period of benefit, not to preserve the original assumption simply because it was once approved. A review at least periodically helps keep the schedule sensible and aligned with reality.

Residual value should also be reviewed when relevant. Some items may have resale value or scrap value; others may have none. That value affects the amount depreciated over time. If the environment changes, a value that once seemed reasonable may no longer be appropriate. The organisation should therefore compare estimates with actual experience, especially for vehicles, IT equipment, and machinery that depreciate rapidly. A controlled review process helps ensure the book value remains meaningful and not just formally correct.

The review should be documented. If the estimate changes, the reason should be explained in the file so later staff can see what changed and why. That makes the depreciation history easier to defend and easier to understand.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- IPSASB — Official site
Q: Why does component accounting matter for complex assets?
A: Show answer
Component accounting matters when an asset has parts that wear out or are replaced at different times. A roof, engine, battery, major medical module, or building structure may not all have the same useful life. If the organisation treats the whole item as one single block, depreciation may become too crude and replacement planning may become misleading. Component accounting allows major parts to be depreciated and replaced according to their own life pattern where policy requires it. That makes the accounting closer to how the asset is actually consumed.

This is especially useful for expensive or technically complex items. If one major part is replaced, the original component may need to be derecognised while the new component is added. That prevents the books from showing both the old and new major parts as if they were the same resource. The process should be driven by materiality and policy, not by unnecessary complexity for minor items. The aim is to make the asset record more truthful, not more complicated for its own sake.

Component accounting also helps maintenance teams and finance teams talk about the same item with the same structure. It is easier to plan when the asset is broken into major parts that actually age differently. That improves repair decisions and replacement forecasts.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- IPSASB — Official site
Q: How should repairs and maintenance be treated compared with capital improvements?
A: Show answer
Repairs and maintenance are usually expensed because they keep an asset in working condition rather than extending it in a way that creates a new future benefit. Capital improvements, by contrast, may be added to the asset cost if they extend useful life, increase capacity, or significantly improve performance. The distinction is important because it changes both the current period result and the future depreciation base. A routine service on a vehicle is normally maintenance; a major rebuild that substantially extends life may be capital. The file should show the logic behind the treatment.

Policy should give staff enough guidance to distinguish between routine upkeep and value-adding work. Without a rule, people may capitalise too much or expense too much. Both errors distort the books. The best control is a clear approval path for unusual repair amounts or major upgrades. That way, large projects cannot be posted casually as repairs just because the invoice arrived that way. The accounting follows the substance of the work, not the label on the supplier invoice.

Maintenance records should also be retained because they explain why the item remained serviceable and may help justify future useful life estimates or impairment reviews.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- GAO Green Book
Q: When should spare parts and standby equipment be tracked as fixed assets rather than inventory?
A: Show answer
Significant spare parts and standby equipment should be tracked as fixed assets when they are expected to be used over more than one period or when they are integral to a specific asset. Ordinary consumable spares may remain inventory, but major engines, backup generators, or critical replacement modules can justify asset treatment depending on the accounting framework and policy. The deciding point is whether the item is a consumable store item or a long-term support resource. If it is meant to remain available over time and is important to operations, it may belong in fixed assets or a separate controlled category.

This area often needs policy clarity because stores and finance may classify the same item differently. The organisation should define what counts as a spare part with long-term significance and who approves the classification. If the item is a standby unit that is kept for emergency use, the file should show whether it is already available for use and whether it should be depreciated. The control objective is to prevent important backup items from being lost between inventory records and asset records.

Operationally important spares should not sit in a grey area. They should be recorded where they can be found, counted, and reviewed consistently.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- IPSASB — Official site
Q: Why should maintenance history be kept with the asset file?
A: Show answer
Maintenance history helps explain why an asset remains usable, why its useful life estimate is still reasonable, and whether a high-value item is being cared for properly. If the organisation only keeps purchase records and depreciation schedules, it may miss the operational story that sits behind the numbers. A machine that has had regular servicing is not the same as one that has been neglected. The maintenance file therefore supports both accounting judgement and operational stewardship. It can also support decisions about replacement timing or whether an asset has become uneconomical to keep.

Maintenance records should show the date, type of service, cost, vendor or internal technician, and whether the item was restored to normal use. For assets with a warranty or service contract, the file should also show what was covered and what was not. That makes later repair decisions easier to evaluate. Good maintenance evidence also helps if the organisation needs to demonstrate proper care to donors, insurers, or auditors. In practical terms, it reduces the chance that people argue about whether the asset failed because it was old, misused, or poorly maintained.

A well-kept maintenance history supports the useful life review process and gives management a better basis for replacement planning.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
Q: When should an impairment review be considered for a fixed asset?
A: Show answer
An impairment review should be considered when there are indicators that an asset may no longer be able to generate the expected service potential or economic benefit. That can happen after damage, obsolescence, significant underuse, a drop in demand, or a change in operating conditions. The point is not simply whether the asset still exists physically. The question is whether the asset still justifies its carrying amount. If the expected benefits have fallen materially, the asset may need to be reviewed under the relevant impairment guidance.

The trigger should be operationally sensible. A cracked machine, an obsolete IT server, or a vehicle that is no longer fit for the intended route may need review even if it has not yet been formally written off. The file should show the indicator, the assessment, the estimated recoverable or service value where required, and the conclusion. Ignoring impairment makes assets look healthier than they are. Reviewing it too casually can lead to understated problems. The organisation should therefore define the signs that require review and who is responsible for escalation.

Impairment is especially relevant where assets are donor-funded or operationally critical, because overstatement can mislead management about actual capacity.

Official links:
- IAS 36 Impairment of Assets — IFRS Foundation
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
Q: Why might revaluation be considered, and why must it be policy-driven?
A: Show answer
Revaluation may be considered where the accounting framework and organisational policy allow it and where the organisation wants carrying amounts to better reflect current values. In some cases, historical cost may become far removed from present value, especially for land or certain long-lived items. However, revaluation is not something to do casually. It changes equity, asset values, and future depreciation patterns. It also requires reliable evidence and consistent application. If done without policy discipline, it can create confusion rather than clarity.

The decision should therefore be framework-based and approved in advance. The organisation should define which asset classes can be revalued, how often valuations should be updated, who performs or reviews the valuation, and how revaluation movements are recognised. A valuation that is not supportable is worse than no valuation at all. The file should show the basis, the date, the valuer or source, and the resulting carrying amount. Staff should be able to explain why the value changed and what accounting effect followed from that change.

Revaluation is a special case, not a routine shortcut. It belongs in a controlled policy environment with clear evidence and review.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- IPSASB — Official site
Q: How should insurance and risk information be linked to the asset register?
A: Show answer
Insurance and risk details help the organisation know which assets are covered, what the coverage limits are, and which items would create the greatest exposure if lost or damaged. High-value or sensitive assets should be easy to identify in the register so that insurance decisions can be made quickly. If the register does not identify the asset clearly, claims become harder to support and renewal planning becomes less reliable. A good register should therefore support both accounting and risk management.

Asset records should show whether the item is insured, the policy number if relevant, the coverage period, and any special exclusions or deductibles that matter to management. That makes it easier to follow up after an incident and to see whether the insurance position still matches the asset base. The register should also help identify assets that may need special security or disaster recovery treatment. This is especially relevant for vehicles, IT equipment, laboratory tools, and other portable or expensive items. The objective is to protect the organisation before a problem occurs, not only after a claim is needed.

Insurance review is not just a finance task. It is part of the broader stewardship responsibility for assets that are difficult or expensive to replace.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- COSO — Official site
Q: How should project-funded assets be managed when the project closes?
A: Show answer
Project-funded assets should be reviewed before closure so the organisation knows whether they stay with the organisation, move to another project, are transferred, or are disposed of under donor conditions. The asset should not simply be left behind because the project budget ended. The register should show the funding source, project code, closing status, and any restriction on future use. If the asset remains in service, responsibility should be reassigned clearly. If it is to be handed over, the transfer should be documented. If it is no longer needed, disposal should follow policy and donor terms.

Project closure is one of the moments when asset control is weakest, because staff are busy closing reports and handover documents. That is why asset review should be built into the closure checklist. Without it, items can disappear into the next phase with no clear accountability. The organisation should therefore treat project end as a formal checkpoint for asset confirmation, not as an administrative afterthought. This is especially important in NGO environments where assets are frequently bought for a specific grant, then later reused across programmes.

The closure file should make it obvious which assets remained, which were transferred, and which were removed so later staff do not have to reconstruct the history from memory.

Official links:
- IPSASB — Official site
- GAO Green Book
Q: Why should the asset register support management reporting, not only audit?
A: Show answer
Because the register is not just a compliance document. It also helps management understand what the organisation owns, what is aging, what is missing, what is underused, and what may need replacement. A good register can support reports on asset age, location concentration, custodial responsibility, maintenance exposure, donor restrictions, and upcoming disposals. That makes it a strategic tool rather than just a ledger of old purchases. The more timely the data, the more useful the report.

Management reporting also becomes more credible when the asset data is stable and traceable. Instead of asking the team to recount everything from scratch, leadership can review the register and ask focused questions about exceptions. That saves time and improves decision quality. It also helps the organisation plan budgets for replacement and maintenance more intelligently. A well-designed register can show patterns that are not obvious in the ledger alone, such as repeated loss in one site or repeated replacement of the same asset type.

When the asset file supports both control and planning, it becomes part of ordinary management practice rather than a once-a-year exercise.

Official links:
- COSO — Official site
- GAO Green Book
Q: What are the most common fixed asset mistakes that should be watched for?
A: Show answer
The most common mistakes are duplicate records, missing tags, wrong locations, omitted depreciation, late disposals, weak supporting documents, and treating repairs as capital improvements or capital items as expenses. Another frequent problem is the register being updated long after the physical event, which creates a gap between the books and the real world. These errors may look small on their own, but they can accumulate and distort both the balance sheet and the control environment. The best defence is a routine process with review points rather than a one-time cleanup.

Another common mistake is assuming that if something is cheap it does not need tracking, or assuming that if something is durable it must always be capitalised. Both assumptions are too simplistic. The right answer depends on policy, purpose, control, and materiality. Staff should therefore be trained to ask the right questions before posting. A short checklist and a clear approval flow can prevent many of these mistakes before they appear in the register. Once errors enter the system, they are often time-consuming to untangle because assets live for years, not days.

The register should be reviewed for patterns, not just single mistakes. Repeated errors usually point to a process weakness rather than isolated human error.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
Q: What is the final practical standard for managing fixed assets well?
A: Show answer
The final practical standard is to keep the physical item, the register, and the source documents aligned from start to finish. If the item exists, it should be traceable; if it is controlled, it should be recorded; if it moves, it should be documented; if it is depreciated, the schedule should be defensible; if it is impaired or lost, the issue should be investigated; and if it leaves service, the record should show exactly how and when that happened. That is the discipline that turns a list of purchases into a proper asset control system.

Well-managed fixed assets do not depend on memory. They depend on repeatable rules, clear ownership, documented movement, regular review, and timely correction. The organisation that keeps those elements in place has better stewardship, better reporting, and better readiness for audit or donor review. The asset register then becomes more than a file. It becomes part of how the organisation governs itself. That is the real purpose of fixed asset accounting in practice: not merely to record cost, but to preserve truth over time and support responsible use of long-term resources.

When the process is disciplined, the asset base becomes easier to trust, easier to explain, and easier to manage.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- GAO Green Book
- IPSASB — Official site
Q: How should a disposal decision be documented when the item is not fully worthless?
A: Show answer
The decision should state why disposal is still appropriate even if the asset retains some residual utility or scrap value. An item does not need to be physically destroyed before it can be disposed of in accounting terms. It may be obsolete, uneconomical to repair, no longer fit for service, or too costly to maintain. The file should explain the condition, the inspection result, the cost of repair versus replacement, and the approved disposal method. That makes the decision defensible later if someone asks why the item was removed when it still looked usable.

If the asset is sold or transferred, the file should also show how value was determined and whether the process was open and approved. If it is scrapped, the file should show why further use was not sensible. The central idea is that disposal follows serviceability and policy, not sentiment. A worn asset may still “work” in a narrow sense, but if it no longer supports the organisation reliably or economically, keeping it on the books can be misleading. Disposal should therefore be based on documented judgement rather than informal impression.

This kind of file helps because many disputes arise not from the disposal itself, but from whether the organisation used a reasonable basis for the decision.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- COSO — Official site
Q: How should asset counts deal with serial numbers, tags, and missing labels?
A: Show answer
Serial numbers and tags should be used together where possible because each solves a different control problem. The tag identifies the organisation’s record; the serial number identifies the manufacturer’s item. If a tag is missing, the serial number may still help confirm identity. If the serial number is missing or obscured, the tag may still link the item to the register. A strong count procedure therefore checks both when available and records exceptions clearly. Missing labels should not be ignored, because they may indicate lost control, improper removal, or a problem with the tagging process itself.

The count sheet should leave room to note unreadable labels, damaged tags, serial number mismatches, and items that look like duplicates. Those exceptions are valuable because they show where the process needs correction. A count that only ticks items off without recording exceptions misses the whole point of the exercise. The count team should also be trained to compare the physical description against the register rather than rely on a single marker. The more identifiers used, the harder it is for an item to be miscounted or misclassified.

Good count practice is not about perfect neatness. It is about making sure each item can be followed from the floor to the register and back again.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
Q: Why should asset custody be assigned to a real person and not only a department?
A: Show answer
Because control becomes stronger when there is a named custodian who can actually confirm receipt, use, and location. A department may be too broad to make accountability practical. A real person creates a clear point of contact for counts, transfers, maintenance, and loss reporting. That does not mean the person is always financially liable in a legal sense. It means the person is responsible for day-to-day stewardship. If the organisation only assigns custody to a unit name, items can drift without anyone feeling responsible for them.

The custodian should know what is expected: protect the item, report changes, allow verification, and not move the asset informally. The register should show the current custodian and any history of prior custodians. That history is valuable because patterns of movement can reveal where control weakens. A named person also helps during audit or investigation because there is a clear route for questions. In practice, real accountability works better than abstract accountability. A clear custodian makes that possible.

When departments change structure, the custodian record should be updated rather than left to decay. The asset remains the same; only the chain of responsibility changes.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- COSO — Official site
Q: Why is documenting the asset location more than a clerical task?
A: Show answer
Location is a control feature, not only a database field. Knowing where an asset is helps with count, maintenance, insurance, security, utilisation, and replacement decisions. If the location is wrong, the organisation may spend time searching, miss a count, or fail to notice that the item has moved without approval. Location also helps identify whether certain sites carry more loss or damage risk than others. A location record that is vague or stale weakens the whole control chain.

The location detail should be specific enough to be useful. “Main office” may not be enough if the item is actually in a floor, room, cabinet, or unit with separate custody. The right level of detail depends on the organisation’s size and risk profile. High-value or sensitive assets need more precise location data than low-value items. The register should also keep a movement history so the current location does not erase the previous one. That history can explain discrepancies later if an item is found somewhere unexpected.

Location control is especially important for organisations with many project sites or shared facilities because those environments create more opportunity for informal movement.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IPSASB — Official site
Q: What should happen when an asset is shared between programmes or projects?
A: Show answer
Shared assets should still have one clear record, even if several programmes use them. The asset should not appear as if it belongs to every programme simultaneously without structure. Instead, the organisation should assign one control owner, then show cost allocation or usage rules if needed. The register should identify the primary custodian, the physical location, and any funding or usage restrictions. If the item is materially used by different projects, the organisation may also need a fair allocation method for maintenance, depreciation, or replacement planning, depending on policy.

Shared use is common in NGO environments, especially for vehicles, printers, meeting equipment, and IT items. The risk is that each team assumes the other team is watching the item. A single control record prevents that. The file should also explain whether the asset can be transferred between programmes, whether it requires special approval to move, and how it should be treated if one project ends. This avoids disputes later and gives management a clear view of who is actually using the item.

Shared assets work best when the register is explicit about control, rather than assuming that shared use equals shared ownership.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- COSO — Official site
Q: Why should fixed asset policies be written down rather than left as habit?
A: Show answer
Because habit varies by person, while policy gives the organisation a stable and defendable rule set. A written policy makes capitalisation thresholds, useful life assumptions, tagging, transfers, counts, impairment triggers, and disposal authority visible to everyone. It reduces arguments because staff can refer to the same rule rather than each person’s interpretation. It also protects the organisation when staff change, because the process remains documented rather than sitting only in one person’s memory.

Policy also helps with training and audit. New staff can learn the expectations faster, and auditors can see that the organisation is applying a consistent framework. A good policy should not be a vague statement of principles only. It should contain practical instructions, exceptions, responsibilities, and approval routes. If the policy is too general, it does not help staff decide what to do. If it is too rigid, it may become hard to use. The balance is to make it detailed enough to guide action but simple enough to follow.

The policy should be reviewed periodically so that it stays aligned with operations, regulations, and the asset profile of the organisation.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IPSASB — Official site
Q: How should the organisation treat assets that are fully depreciated but still in use?
A: Show answer
Fully depreciated does not mean useless. It means the accounting cost has already been allocated across the useful life estimate. If the asset is still operating, it should remain on the register until it is actually disposed of, impaired, or written off. Removing it just because book value reached zero would make the register less truthful. The organisation still needs to know that the item exists, where it is, who uses it, and whether it requires maintenance. A zero net book value item can still be important operationally.

The file should also show whether the useful life estimate should have been longer, whether the asset is now beyond its original plan, and whether a replacement budget is needed. A fully depreciated asset still tells management something useful about the age and health of the asset base. If it is still essential, the register should make that obvious. If it is no longer useful, disposal should follow. In either case, book value alone should never be the only reason to remove the item from control.

This principle helps prevent the common mistake of treating accounting life and operational life as the same thing.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- GAO Green Book
Q: Why should asset disposals be approved before the item actually leaves control?
A: Show answer
Because approval beforehand prevents the organisation from losing the ability to verify what was removed and why. If an item disappears first and the paperwork comes later, the process becomes harder to trust. Approval before disposal ensures that the condition, method, and destination have been reviewed before the asset is released. That is especially important for valuable items, donor-funded assets, and items with resale potential. The approval trail creates a clear point at which responsibility changes from active custody to disposal handling.

Pre-approval also helps stop informal sale, scrap, or donation of organisational property. It protects against conflict of interest and prevents staff from acting unilaterally on items that belong to the organisation. The file should show who approved, when they approved, what method was chosen, and whether any restriction applied. Once the approval is in place, the physical movement can be recorded with confidence. That sequencing keeps the books, the approval trail, and the physical event aligned.

In practice, the best disposal control is the one that makes it hard for items to disappear quietly before anyone knows.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- COSO — Official site
Q: Why should asset files retain the original acquisition evidence?
A: Show answer
Because acquisition evidence explains the origin, cost, and basis of the asset record. Without the original invoice, donation note, purchase order, or acceptance document, later staff may not be able to prove why the item was capitalised, what amount should be recorded, or whether the classification was correct. The original evidence is the first link in the asset lifecycle. If that link is weak, every later step becomes harder to justify. The register should therefore point back to the source record, not exist in isolation.

This is especially important when an asset is added through multiple costs such as delivery, installation, testing, or customs. The original file should show the full costing logic. It also helps if the item is later sold or written off, because the original evidence allows the organisation to trace the full lifecycle. Over time, people forget why something was posted in a certain way. The source evidence protects against that memory loss. It is one of the simplest ways to make the register defendable many years later.

A clean register without supporting evidence is only half a control system. The evidence is what makes the register credible.

Official links:
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
- GAO Green Book
Q: Why do asset counts and asset reconciliations need different checks?
A: Show answer
A count confirms physical existence, while reconciliation confirms that the physical existence agrees with the accounting record. Counting tells the organisation what is present; reconciliation tells the organisation whether the present item is recorded correctly, at the right value, location, custodian, and status. One check without the other is incomplete. An item may be present but wrongly recorded, or recorded correctly but missing physically. Both conditions matter. That is why the process should have both a physical and a financial stage.

During reconciliation, the organisation should compare the count results to the register, then the register to the general ledger if relevant, and then the supporting documents to both. That sequence helps isolate where an error started. If the count is wrong, the register may be right. If the register is wrong, the ledger may still be right or wrong depending on posting. Reconciliation makes those paths visible. It prevents the organisation from assuming that because numbers balance, the system is accurate. Accuracy needs evidence, not just arithmetic.

The stronger the reconciliation routine, the easier it becomes to prevent future differences because the root cause becomes visible faster.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- COSO — Official site
Q: How should an asset handover between project phases be recorded?
A: Show answer
The handover should record what is being handed over, from whom, to whom, why it is moving, and what condition it is in at the transfer date. That matters because project transitions are one of the most common moments when items are lost or incorrectly retained. A handover note should also identify whether the item remains within the same programme, moves to another programme, or transfers to central control. The note should match the actual movement rather than a verbal understanding of it.

Handover documentation should be signed by both sides if possible. If equipment is being passed from one team to another, the register should be updated immediately so the chain of custody is not lost. If the item has attachments, accessories, or related manuals, these should be listed too. That prevents disputes about whether the receiving team accepted the full set. Good handover records are particularly useful when people later ask why an item was found in a different unit or site than expected. The record provides the answer before the memory fades.

A good handover file is a simple but effective way to stop project changes from becoming asset loss.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IPSASB — Official site
Q: What should the organisation do when a physical asset exists but the register is missing the item?
A: Show answer
The organisation should investigate before creating a new record blindly. It should confirm whether the item was never recorded, whether it was transferred from another unit, whether it should have been written off earlier, or whether it belongs to a category that was intentionally excluded. The goal is to avoid duplicate records while restoring control. A physical item without a record is a control gap, but solving it incorrectly can create a bigger one. The investigation should therefore determine identity, ownership, location, and funding basis before anything is posted.

If the item is confirmed as organisational property and no prior record exists, the organisation should create a record with clear supporting evidence and an explanation of how the omission occurred. That prevents the same error from happening again. If the item belongs to another party or to a different project, the register should show that clearly instead. Either way, the register and the physical world should be brought back together in a documented way. Creating a clean trail after the fact is better than leaving the item outside control simply because the original step was missed.

This situation is also useful as a control lesson because it often shows where the acquisition or handover process was weak.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- COSO — Official site
Q: What should happen when the register contains an item that no longer exists physically?
A: Show answer
The item should not remain on the books just because the paperwork was never caught up. The organisation should verify whether the item was lost, stolen, transferred, disposed of, scrapped, or simply miscounted. Once the cause is known, the register should be corrected and supported by the appropriate documentation. Leaving a non-existent asset on the register overstates resources and can distort insurance, replacement planning, and financial reporting. The correction should be done formally, not by casual deletion, so that the history remains available.

If the asset cannot be located and no evidence of approved disposal exists, the organisation should treat it as an exception requiring review. A loss report, incident note, or investigation memo may be necessary. That way, the write-off or removal has a documented basis. The key point is that the books should not keep describing an item that is no longer under control. Good governance demands that the record reflects actual existence, not wishful continuity.

Whenever a mismatch occurs, the organisation should ask what process failed rather than only fixing the number.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- COSO — Official site
Q: Why should a final asset standard be simple enough for staff to repeat?
A: Show answer
Because a standard that is too complicated will not be followed consistently. The best fixed asset control is detailed enough to protect the organisation but simple enough for staff to apply in ordinary work. If the rule can be repeated across offices, projects, and staffing changes, it becomes part of the organisation’s operating discipline. The standard should therefore be practical: identify the item, approve the acquisition, classify it correctly, tag it, assign custody, update the register, count it, depreciate it, and dispose of it with evidence. That sequence is easier to follow than a collection of vague principles.

A repeatable standard also makes training and handover easier. New staff can understand what to do, and current staff can apply the same logic to each transaction. That reduces variation and improves audit results. The aim is not to make asset control fancy. The aim is to make it reliable. Simplicity is valuable when it preserves the discipline of control without forcing staff into unnecessary complexity. A repeatable standard is usually the best sign that the process has matured.

When the procedure is simple, documented, and enforced, the organisation can keep the register truthful without turning each asset into a special case.

Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- IAS 16 — IFRS Foundation
Rows: 57
Table 7
Notes
Programme Advances (Floats)

1. Overview and Purpose

Programme advances, also called floats, are short-term cash advances issued to staff or other authorised holders so they can meet legitimate programme-related costs where immediate payment by supplier transfer, bank transfer, card payment, or another normal settlement method is not practical. They exist to support operations, not to bypass procurement discipline, budgeting rules, or basic financial control. A float is therefore a controlled temporary funding arrangement, not a convenience fund and not an open-ended entitlement. Its purpose is to allow work to proceed in situations where the normal payment cycle would delay an approved activity, create hardship in the field, or prevent a time-sensitive programme event from taking place on schedule.

The real value of a float is that it helps the organisation respond quickly while still preserving traceability. In a healthy system, every advance can be traced from request to approval, from issue to use, and from use to final settlement. That traceability matters because cash is inherently exposed once it leaves the office. The moment funds are handed over, the organisation relies on the holder’s honesty, discipline, and documentation habits. For that reason, programme advances should always be issued with clear conditions, a defined business purpose, a reasonable settlement deadline, and a strong expectation that the cash will be accounted for in full. If the advance cannot be settled promptly and clearly, then the arrangement has ceased to function as a control and has instead become an unmanaged exposure.

It is also important to understand the practical role of a float in day-to-day operations. Many programme activities involve dispersed locations, limited banking access, urgent field conditions, or beneficiary-facing work where the cost is too small or too immediate to route through a full payment cycle each time. In those cases, the advance is a working tool that bridges the gap between the approved activity and the moment the expenditure can be evidenced. That bridge is only safe if the office treats it as temporary. The float is not supposed to become part of the organisation’s permanent funding structure. When it is used correctly, it helps staff act quickly without weakening the integrity of the accounting system. When it is used carelessly, it becomes a source of missing documentation, delayed settlement, unexplained balances, and eventually a breakdown in trust between Finance, Programme, and management.

2. Scope and Applicability

This policy applies to all offices, teams, field locations, project sites, emergency operations, and temporary work settings where programme advances may be needed. It applies whether the advance is issued in physical cash, transferred into a controlled account for temporary use, or provided through another arrangement that gives one person or unit access to organisational funds before final expenditure documentation is complete. It also applies to operational scenarios where normal payment channels are unavailable, unreliable, too slow, or incompatible with the timing of the activity. In practice, this means the rules cover office-based work, field outreach, travel, emergency response, local disbursement exercises, partner support, and any other setting where a temporary cash advance is used to support an approved activity.

The scope is intentionally broad because control weaknesses often appear in the places that feel exceptional. In routine operations, there is usually time to obtain approvals and process payments properly. In emergency or field conditions, however, people may be tempted to relax controls because speed is urgent and documentation appears secondary. That is precisely when the policy matters most. A difficult environment does not remove the need for accountability; it increases it. The same basic logic applies in every location: the advance must have a legitimate purpose, a responsible holder, a defined ceiling, supporting evidence, and a settlement path that returns the money or reconciles it without delay. Where local circumstances require special treatment, that treatment should be defined in writing rather than improvised informally.

The policy should also be read alongside any related rules on procurement, travel, per diem, field operations, petty cash, grants, partner disbursements, safeguarding, anti-fraud, and delegated authority. A programme advance may interact with all of those areas, but it does not override them. For example, a float used for field transport does not create a right to ignore vendor selection rules, and a cash advance used for community work does not create a right to skip beneficiary documentation. The policy exists to coordinate those different rules, not replace them. In a disciplined office, the advance procedure should fit into the wider control framework rather than sitting outside it as an informal exception.

3. Control Objectives

The first control objective is to minimise the amount of cash exposed to risk at any one time. That means advances should be issued only when needed, in amounts that are no larger than necessary, and for periods that are no longer than necessary. Every extra day that money remains unsettled creates additional exposure. Every extra currency unit issued without justification increases the chance of loss, misuse, or reconciliation problems. The policy therefore seeks to keep floats small, targeted, and short-lived. That discipline improves both security and reporting quality, because an organisation that settles advances quickly can understand its true cash position more accurately.

The second control objective is to ensure that each float is pre-authorised and linked to a valid programme activity. The amount should match the approved budget, the holder should be identified, and the timing should be realistic. The third objective is accurate accounting. The ledger should reflect what actually happened, not what the holder intended to happen, and not what the team hoped would happen. If the cash was spent on five small items, the accounting should show those five items. If only part of the advance was used, the balance should remain visible until returned or cleared. The fourth objective is consistency. Similar transactions should be treated in similar ways across offices so that management can compare balances, identify patterns, and respond to exceptions without having to decode local habits or undocumented workarounds.

The fifth objective is accountability over convenience. A float system should make it easy to act quickly when there is a real operational need, but not easy to use cash casually. That distinction matters because many control failures begin when staff start treating a temporary tool as if it were a normal spending account. The sixth objective is recoverability. If a holder does not settle, loses supporting evidence, or spends outside the approved purpose, the organisation should still be able to determine what was issued, what remains open, and what action must follow. The system should never rely on memory alone. Once cash has moved, the record must be strong enough to defend the transaction months later if required by management, auditors, donors, or internal review. These control objectives define not only how the float should work, but also what success looks like.

4. Governance and Accountability

Programme advances depend on clear ownership. The advance holder is the person directly responsible for receiving, safeguarding, using, and settling the funds. That person must request only what is needed, spend only on approved items, retain all supporting evidence, and return any unused balance. The budget holder or programme manager is responsible for confirming that the activity is legitimate and that the amount requested aligns with the approved workplan or budget. Finance is responsible for recording the transaction, maintaining the register, monitoring ageing, checking completeness, and following up on overdue or unsupported items. Senior management is responsible for enforcing discipline, resolving escalations, and approving exceptions only where policy explicitly permits them. These roles should not blur together, because when everyone is responsible, no one is truly responsible.

Governance must remain visible. If the same person requests, receives, spends, and approves without independent review, the risk of error and misuse increases sharply. Separation of duties does not require a large team, but it does require some form of independent challenge. Where staffing is limited, compensating controls should be used. These may include secondary approval above a threshold, periodic review by a supervisor not involved in the transaction, mandatory document checks before issue, and monthly scrutiny of the ageing report. The purpose of governance is not to create paperwork for its own sake. It is to make each stage of the float visible to someone other than the person benefiting from it, so that irregularities do not disappear into routine processing.

Governance also means accepting that the float process is part of the organisation’s control environment, not a side activity. If the office permits repeated exceptions without escalation, the policy gradually loses force. If the office allows unresolved balances to linger because the holder is busy or well-liked, the perception of fairness weakens. If Finance is expected to chase all overdue items without support from Programme, accountability becomes fragmented. Good governance therefore requires steady coordination across functions. The float process should produce a clear chain of responsibility that can be followed from approval through to final posting. That chain is what makes the process audit-ready and operationally reliable.

5. Roles and Responsibilities

The advance holder is the first line of control. That person must treat the money as organisational funds, not personal cash. The holder should keep the advance secure, avoid mixing it with personal money, use it only for the approved purpose, and collect receipts, invoices, beneficiary signatures, and other proof of expenditure as spending occurs. Waiting until the end of the trip or activity to reconstruct evidence usually leads to missing items, weak explanations, and disputed balances. The holder should also return unused cash promptly and submit the settlement package by the required deadline. If the package is incomplete, the default position should be that the advance remains open until the gap is explained and resolved through the proper route.

Finance is responsible for the system of record. That includes maintaining the advance register, entering the issue in the ledger, checking for prior unresolved balances, monitoring due dates, and preparing the settlement entries once valid evidence is received. Finance should not issue a new advance to someone who still has a materially overdue prior advance unless there is documented exception approval. Programme or operational management is responsible for confirming that the advance supports the intended activity and for helping resolve delays when a holder is not responding. Senior finance leadership should review recurring exceptions, repeated late settlements, and any pattern that suggests a weakness in the control culture. The combined effect of these roles should be a process in which no one can move cash through the system without leaving a trace and without another person being able to verify the movement.

Supervisors also have a practical responsibility beyond formal sign-off. They should know whether their team members are routinely settling on time and whether the advance is actually appropriate for the activity being carried out. A supervisor who approves advances but never checks outcomes is only half fulfilling the role. Likewise, a finance team that records balances but does not challenge repeated lateness is allowing the control cycle to weaken. The responsibility structure should therefore be active, not ceremonial. Each role exists because the others need it. If any part of the chain becomes passive, the float system becomes vulnerable to avoidable loss, unresolved receivables, and poor reporting quality.

6. Authorisation, Eligibility, and Limits

Every advance must be authorised before funds are issued. Verbal approval may be used only in a genuine emergency and only if it is documented and confirmed as soon as practicable under the organisation’s emergency approval process. The request should identify the purpose, amount, currency, expected settlement date, budget line or programme activity, and named holder. It should also explain why normal payment methods are not practical. This matters because the approver is not merely authorising a cash movement; the approver is accepting that the activity is legitimate, the amount is reasonable, and the proposed control path is defensible. Without a written basis, the advance becomes hard to justify later and difficult to defend in review or audit.

Eligibility should also be defined. Advances are usually appropriate only for staff or other clearly authorised individuals who can be held accountable through an organisational process. They should not be issued casually to external vendors, contractors, or persons whose relationship to the organisation makes recovery or settlement difficult. The organisation should define thresholds. A common control is to cap the amount relative to the holder’s remuneration, the expected activity cost, or another locally approved benchmark. The exact limit may differ by office, but the principle is constant: the amount should be proportionate and defensible. As a general rule, one person should normally have only one unresolved advance at a time. Multiple open advances create confusion, weaken accountability, and make collection much harder if the holder becomes unreachable or if the activity changes unexpectedly.

Limits should be reviewed periodically rather than left static forever. A limit that was sensible for a small field operation may be too low for a larger programme phase, while a limit that was set generously during a crisis may remain too high long after the emergency has passed. The office should compare actual usage to the approved limits and ask whether the ceiling is being used as intended. If every request is at or near the maximum, the threshold may need review. If several people repeatedly need more than the cap allows, the control may be forcing the wrong workflow. In that case, management should consider whether the activity should continue to use floats at all or whether a different payment method would provide better discipline. Thresholds should serve the activity, not merely preserve a historical rule.

7. Request, Review, and Issuance Process

The request process should begin with a completed advance form or equivalent authorised request. That form should state the activity, purpose, expected dates, estimated cost, beneficiary details where cash will be distributed, and the person who will hold the funds. The budget holder should verify that the request is consistent with the approved programme and that the amount is sensible. Finance should then confirm that the holder has no overdue balance, no unresolved documentation gap, and no conflict with policy. Only after those checks should the approver sign off. This sequence matters because approving first and checking later creates a weak control environment in which repeated problems can slip through unnoticed.

Before funds are released, finance should assign a unique reference number and record the issuance in the finance system and advance register. The issue record should include the amount, currency, holder, purpose, date issued, expected settlement date, and approving authority. The cash or bank transfer should then be handed over using a controlled process, ideally with a signed acknowledgement, transfer confirmation, or cash count signed by both parties. The person issuing the funds should not rely on memory or informal notes. A clean issue record is what makes later tracing possible. If the money leaves the office without a unique reference, a named holder, and a dated record, then the organisation is immediately at a disadvantage when trying to prove what happened or recover an unpaid balance.

The issuance process should also include a final reasonableness check. The reviewer should ask whether the amount is truly needed, whether the holder is the right person, whether the activity timing makes sense, and whether the documentation package is complete enough to support the advance. A request that appears technically correct may still be poor in substance if the amount is inflated, the purpose is vague, or the activity itself is poorly defined. Issuance should therefore be the last point where the organisation can still safely say no. Once the cash has left, the control burden becomes much higher. A disciplined issue process prevents a small error from becoming a later recovery problem.

8. Accounting Treatment and Ledger Recording

All programme advances should be recorded in the finance system at the time of issue. The normal accounting treatment is to debit a staff advance or programme advance account and credit cash or bank, depending on how the money was released. The account used should be consistent across the organisation so that outstanding balances can be reviewed, aged, and reconciled centrally. The narrative should identify the advance reference, the holder, the programme, and the expected return date. This helps anyone reviewing the ledger understand what the balance represents without needing to search elsewhere for context. A well-labelled ledger is part of the control, not just a bookkeeping convenience.

When the advance is settled, the finance team should credit the advance account and debit the relevant expense accounts based on the approved supporting documents. If unused cash is returned, that return should be recorded separately and linked clearly to the original issue. Where part of the advance cannot be supported by valid documentation, the amount should not simply be forced into expense to make the balance disappear.
