Documented disclosure and review provide evidence that the organisation did not ignore the risk. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please disclose and review any conflict of interest before choosing the partner or vendor.”
Key principle:
- Conflicts must be disclosed, not assumed away.
- Review and recusal are control actions.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.403 Factors affecting allowability of costs
- GAO Green Book
Q: Why should a partner capacity-building plan have milestones and evidence requirements?
A: Show answer
Capacity-building without milestones often becomes vague mentoring that cannot be measured. A good plan should have specific actions, deadlines, evidence of completion, and clear ownership. For example, a partner may need bank reconciliation training, revised filing procedures, fixed asset tagging, or support in building a cash-flow report. Each action should produce evidence such as a training attendance record, a revised template, or a sample reconciliation signed by the responsible staff member. If the plan is tied to milestones, the organisation can tell whether capacity has truly improved rather than merely being discussed. The purpose of the plan is to reduce risk and improve sustainability, not to create another file that no one revisits. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please make sure every capacity-building action has a deadline and evidence of completion.”
Key principle:
- Improvement must be measurable.
- Training is only useful when evidence exists.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
- GAO Green Book
Q: How can the organisation use analytics to detect irregular spending patterns in partner files?
A: Show answer
Analytics can be used to compare spending by period, vendor, budget line, staff member, and geography. The team can look for repeated round amounts, concentration of spend near period-end, frequent split invoices, duplicate descriptions, or sudden jumps in one category that are not explained by the workplan. The point is not to accuse the partner automatically. The point is to generate questions that humans can then review with evidence. Even a simple spreadsheet can highlight unusual trends when the same logic is applied consistently across partners. Analytics are most useful when they turn a large file into a short exception list. That lets the team review high-risk items first instead of scanning everything manually. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please review any unusual vendor pattern, round amount, or end-period spike before approving the report.”
Key principle:
- Analytics should surface exceptions, not replace judgment.
- Unusual patterns deserve explanation.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
- 2 CFR 200.332 Requirements for pass-through entities
Q: What should happen if the partner asks to change the payment frequency or advance size?
A: Show answer
The request should be evaluated based on risk, utilisation, reporting quality, and the remaining period of performance. A partner that is spending slowly may not need larger or more frequent advances. A partner that is spending too quickly may need tighter monitoring, not a bigger cash balance. Any change should be backed by cash-flow analysis and approved by the right authority before the new schedule starts. The organisation should not change the payment rhythm casually, because payment frequency is part of the control structure. If the frequency is loosened without reason, the partner can accumulate excess cash. If it is tightened too much, implementation may stall. The right answer is evidence-based, not habitual. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please review any request to change payment frequency or advance size against utilisation and risk.”
Key principle:
- Payment rhythm is a control decision.
- Cash size should match actual need.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.328 Financial reporting
- GAO Green Book
Q: Why should a partner file include a single master timeline of approvals, reports, and payments?
A: Show answer
A master timeline helps the organisation see the full sequence of the award in one place. It should show when the partner was selected, when the assessment was completed, when the agreement was signed, when each payment was made, when each report was due, when monitoring occurred, when findings were cleared, and when close-out happened. Without a timeline, the file becomes a stack of documents without context. With a timeline, reviewers can immediately see whether a payment happened too early, a report was late, or an amendment came after the fact. The timeline is therefore a practical control tool, not just a reference page. It allows finance, programme, audit, and management to read the same history in the same order. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please keep one master timeline for each partner award.”
Key principle:
- Sequence matters as much as content.
- One timeline makes review faster and clearer.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
- 2 CFR 200.334 Record retention requirements
Q: How should the organisation deal with repeated late reporting by a partner?
A: Show answer
Repeated late reporting should trigger a structured escalation path. First, the team should confirm whether the delay is caused by missing source documents, weak internal controls, staffing issues, or a genuine operational emergency. Second, the organisation should record the delay history and revise the monitoring plan. Third, it should consider withholding future tranches, reducing the size of advances, or requiring a corrective action plan with deadlines. Late reporting is not only an administrative annoyance; it can hide spending problems and delay the detection of ineligible costs. If the organisation keeps paying on time while reports remain late, it is effectively rewarding weak compliance. The response should therefore be consistent, documented, and proportionate to the pattern. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please escalate repeated late reporting and consider tighter cash control if the pattern continues.”
Key principle:
- Repeated lateness should change the control response.
- Escalation should be documented.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.332 Requirements for pass-through entities
- GAO Green Book
Q: Why is a standard reporting template important for partners?
A: Show answer
A standard template makes partner data easier to compare, review, and consolidate. If every partner uses a different format, the finance team spends time remapping columns, chasing missing fields, and correcting inconsistent labels. A standard template should make it clear what periods are covered, what budget lines are being reported, what supporting documents are required, and how exceptions should be explained. It should also include clear instructions on which line items need narrative explanation and which require supporting attachments. The template is a control device because it shapes the quality of the data before it reaches the finance review stage. If the template is good, the review becomes faster and more focused. If the template is poor, even strong finance staff will spend time cleaning basic errors instead of checking real risk. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please use the standard report template for every partner submission.”
Key principle:
- Consistent templates improve control and comparison.
- Reporting quality starts at the input stage.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.328 Financial reporting
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
Q: How should the organisation respond to unsupported or questionable costs in a partner report?
A: Show answer
Questionable costs should not be accepted just because the partner included them in a report. The organisation should ask for the missing support, test whether the cost is allowable and allocable, and confirm whether the charge falls inside the approved scope and period. If the partner cannot provide evidence, the cost may need to be disallowed, reduced, reclassified, or recovered depending on the circumstances. The response should be documented clearly so the partner knows what correction is needed. A structured query log can be helpful here because it records the question, the response, the deadline, and the final disposition. Unsupported costs are a warning sign because they can be symptoms of weak accounting, weak procurement, or weak programme oversight. The organisation should not normalise them. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please query any unsupported cost and do not approve it until the evidence is complete.”
Key principle:
- Unsupported costs need formal resolution.
- Allowability depends on evidence.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.403 Factors affecting allowability of costs
- 2 CFR 200.405 Allocable costs
Q: Why should a partner’s financial report be reconciled to the bank before approval?
A: Show answer
Bank reconciliation proves that the reported cash balance is real and that the transactions in the report can be traced to actual bank movement. If the partner report is approved without reconciliation, the organisation may miss unrecorded withdrawals, duplicate receipts, uncleared payments, or wrong balances. The reconciliation should show the closing bank balance, outstanding items, timing differences, and any unexplained differences. Finance should not approve a report simply because the totals look reasonable. Bank evidence is one of the strongest controls because it comes from outside the partner’s own records. When cash is involved, the report and the bank must tell the same story. If they do not, the issue should be resolved before payment or close-out. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please reconcile the partner’s report to the bank before approval.”
Key principle:
- Bank reconciliation is mandatory before reliance.
- Report and bank evidence must match.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.328 Financial reporting
- GAO Green Book
Q: What should happen when a partner wants to reprogram budget between lines?
A: Show answer
Budget reprogramming should follow the terms of the agreement and the significance of the change. If the movement is minor and within delegated authority, it may be approved at the operational level. If the movement changes the activity emphasis, weakens the original objective, or shifts money into an unapproved category, it should require higher review. The organisation should also consider whether the change affects risk, reporting, or performance monitoring. A reprogramming request should explain the reason, the amount, the original line, the destination line, and the expected effect on outputs. The key is that budget movement must support the award, not simply reflect convenience. A good approval memo creates a permanent record of the decision and its rationale. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please review any budget reprogramming request in line with the agreement and approval limits.”
Key principle:
- Budget movement should have a documented rationale.
- Approval level should match the size and impact of the change.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.332 Requirements for pass-through entities
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
Q: How should the organisation treat indirect costs or overhead in a sub-award?
A: Show answer
Indirect costs or overhead should only be charged if the agreement allows them and the basis is clearly stated. The organisation should know whether the rate is fixed, capped, negotiated, or allocated by another method. If the partner charges overhead, finance should verify whether it was budgeted correctly and whether the calculation matches the agreed rate. Overhead should not be used as a catch-all line for unidentified expenses. The treatment should also be consistent across periods so that the partner cannot shift unexplained costs into indirect charges after the fact. Good overhead control protects both the programme and the funder by keeping actual direct costs clean and the shared cost methodology transparent. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please verify the overhead rate and calculation before approving indirect charges.”
Key principle:
- Overhead must be authorised and calculated correctly.
- Indirect charges should not hide unsupported costs.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.405 Allocable costs
- 2 CFR 200.403 Factors affecting allowability of costs
Q: Why is a central sub-award register so useful?
A: Show answer
A central register gives the organisation one place to see every active partner, award number, reporting date, payment status, risk level, amendment history, advance balance, outstanding action, and close-out stage. Without a central register, those facts are usually scattered across email threads, spreadsheets, and staff memory. That makes it hard to answer simple questions such as which partner is overdue, which award is close to expiry, or which report is blocking the next payment. A good register becomes the front page of the entire management process. It helps finance, programme, audit, and management work from the same facts rather than from parallel notes. For a growing portfolio, the register is one of the fastest ways to improve control and visibility at the same time. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please keep one central sub-award register for all active partners.”
Key principle:
- One register improves visibility and control.
- Shared facts reduce duplicated tracking.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
- 2 CFR 200.332 Requirements for pass-through entities
Q: What information should the register show for each partner award?
A: Show answer
The register should show the partner name, award reference, agreement date, start and end dates, budget value, cumulative payments, current advance or settlement balance, risk rating, last report date, next due date, outstanding findings, and close-out status. It should also show whether the agreement has been amended and whether the partner is under a corrective action plan. If possible, the register should separate financial and programme due dates so staff can see whether the delay is on the cash side or the narrative side. A good register is not just a list. It is a control dashboard that supports reminders, escalation, and monthly review. The more complete the field structure, the less staff rely on memory or informal follow-up. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please keep the partner register complete and update it after every report and payment.”
Key principle:
- The register should support action, not just storage.
- Financial and programme dates should be visible together.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.332 Requirements for pass-through entities
- GAO Green Book
Q: How can reminders and escalation help partner compliance?
A: Show answer
Automatic reminders are useful because many compliance failures start as simple delays. A partner may forget a report deadline, a monitoring action, a bank reconciliation, or a response to a query. If the organisation has a structured reminder cycle, most delays can be fixed before they become formal issues. Escalation then comes into play if the reminder is ignored or if the same issue repeats. The reminder system should be simple, visible, and dated, so the team knows exactly when follow-up started. It should also record who sent the reminder and what the response was. Reminders are not about nagging; they are about creating a reliable chain of accountability so that tasks do not disappear between meetings. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please use the reminder and escalation log so overdue items do not get lost.”
Key principle:
- Timely reminders prevent small delays from becoming control failures.
- Escalation should follow a documented sequence.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.303 Internal controls
- GAO Green Book
Q: Why should sub-award data be stored with restricted access?
A: Show answer
Sub-award files often contain bank details, staff information, salary data, donor restrictions, procurement evidence, and sometimes audit findings or allegations. That information should not be broadly visible to everyone in the organisation. Access should therefore be role-based and limited to staff who need the information to perform their duties. Restricting access reduces the chance of unauthorised disclosure, internal misuse, or accidental editing of critical records. The file structure should also protect version integrity so that no one can quietly overwrite the final copy. Secure storage is not only an IT preference; it is a finance control because sensitive records can affect payment decisions, partner trust, and the defensibility of the award file. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please keep sub-award files in restricted folders with role-based access.”
Key principle:
- Sensitive financial data should be need-to-know only.
- File integrity matters as much as confidentiality.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.337 Access to records
- 2 CFR 200.334 Record retention requirements
Q: What should a final review pack for a sub-award include before sign-off?
A: Show answer
The final review pack should confirm that the award file is complete, the final narrative and financial reports have been accepted, bank and cash balances are reconciled, any repayments or unspent funds are accounted for, outstanding findings are resolved or documented, asset treatment is complete, and all approval trails are saved. The pack should also include the final risk assessment, any amendment history, monitoring reports, and the close-out checklist. Senior finance or delegated management should sign off only after the pack shows that the award has reached a clean end state. A strong final review pack prevents the most common close-out problem, which is leaving a file technically open even though staff have already moved on to other work. (eCFR)
Calm reminder to send to the team:
- “Please sign off only when the final review pack proves the award is complete.”
Key principle:
- Final sign-off requires a complete evidence pack.
- Open issues must be resolved or clearly documented.
Authoritative links:
- 2 CFR 200.334 Record retention requirements
- 2 CFR 200.337 Access to records
Rows: 39
Table 17
Notes
Debtors, Creditors and Bad Debts

1. Overview

Debtors, creditors and bad debts are not separate administrative issues. They are one connected control area that affects cash flow, profit recognition, liability completeness, balance sheet accuracy, operational accountability and the organisation’s ability to prove what it is owed and what it owes. A debtor balance represents a claim to future cash or offset. A creditor balance represents a present or future obligation. A bad debt is a debtor balance that has lost enough recoverability to no longer deserve full carrying value. The basic definitions sound simple, but the control environment surrounding them is where the difficulty begins.

In many organisations, these balances are treated as routine ledger items and therefore receive less scrutiny than they should. That is a mistake. The debtor ledger is often where weak invoicing, missing support, poor follow-up, delayed escalation and unrealistic optimism first become visible. The creditor ledger is often where duplicate payments, unapplied invoices, stale liabilities, unmatched receipts and unresolved disputes begin to accumulate. If neither side is monitored with discipline, the organisation can appear solvent on paper while hiding collections risk, payment risk or both.

The practical purpose of debtor and creditor management is to keep the accounting records aligned with reality. If a balance exists, it should be valid. If it is valid, it should be current, aged, followed up, reconciled, corrected or settled according to evidence. If it is no longer collectible, it should be provisioned or written off with approval. If it is no longer payable, it should be reversed or cleared. The objective is not cosmetic neatness. The objective is trustworthy financial information and disciplined cash management.

Bad debts complete the cycle because they force the organisation to confront the limits of collection. Not every overdue amount is collectible, and not every delay is temporary. Some balances are disputed, some are delayed by administrative issues, some are blocked by funding conditions, and some are genuinely irrecoverable. The organisation must distinguish between these states. If it cannot do that, it will either understate losses or overstate assets, and both errors distort management judgement.

2. Key Definitions and Classification

A debtor is any party that owes money to the organisation. That may be a customer, donor, member, partner, employee, statutory authority, related party, or another organisation with an outstanding obligation. In accounting terms, debtor balances are usually recorded in accounts receivable or a similar control account. The balance may arise from an invoice, a reimbursement claim, a recoverable advance, a project entitlement, a tax refund, a recharge, or any other transaction that creates a right to future cash or offset. The key feature is that the organisation has a genuine claim that can be supported by evidence.

A creditor is the reverse. It is a party to whom the organisation owes money, usually recorded in accounts payable or another liability account. The balance may arise from a supplier invoice, staff expense claim, accrual, statutory deduction, partner liability, inter-company recharge, service contract, or any other obligation that the organisation has recognised and expects to settle. Not every payment request is a valid creditor. The balance must reflect an actual obligation, not merely an unprocessed claim or an unauthorised request.

A normal debtor is one that is expected to be collected within the agreed terms. A doubtful debtor is one whose collection is uncertain in whole or in part. A bad debt is one that is judged irrecoverable after reasonable follow-up and review. These classifications are important because they determine how the balance is presented, how aggressively it is followed up, and whether it should be provisioned or written off. A balance can move from normal to doubtful and eventually to bad, but that movement should be driven by evidence rather than by age alone.

Provisioning and write-off are separate ideas. A provision is an estimate of expected loss. It reduces the carrying amount of receivables while still keeping the item on the books. A write-off removes the balance because the organisation has concluded that the amount is no longer recoverable in any meaningful sense. Provisioning is about expected future loss. Write-off is about recognised realised loss. Confusing the two causes weak reporting and poor governance because it hides whether the loss was anticipated or only discovered late.

Ageing bands are the standard way to classify the passage of time. A balance may be current, 1–30 days overdue, 31–60 days overdue, 61–90 days overdue or 90+ days overdue. Some organisations use finer bands, especially when balances are high or the risk profile is difficult. Age is not proof of collectability or irrecoverability, but it is an important signal. A current balance can still be bad if the underlying entitlement is weak; an old balance can still be collectible if the counterparty is active and the dispute is valid. Age should therefore be reviewed alongside other indicators.

Foreign currency classification matters as well. A balance may be created in one currency and reported in another. In that case the original currency, the exchange rate, the reporting currency equivalent and any exchange movement must all be visible. Open foreign currency balances carry both credit risk and exchange risk. If the organisation does not track both, it may underestimate the total exposure.

3. Entitlement, Recognition and Timing

The first control question is whether the organisation truly has the right to recognise the balance. A receivable should only be recognised when the right to consideration or reimbursement has been earned and can be demonstrated through documentary evidence. That evidence may include a signed contract, purchase order, award letter, service completion certificate, milestone sign-off, delivery note, donor instruction, approved claim, or another enforceable basis. The organisation should not record a debtor merely because it expects to be paid eventually. Expectation is not enough; entitlement must exist.

Recognition should follow substance, not convenience. If a service has only partly been delivered, only the delivered part should be recognised if the contractual terms permit that treatment. If a milestone is only partly complete, only the earned portion should be recorded. If a reimbursement claim is pending approval, it should not be forced into the ledger before the required conditions have been met. The same principle applies in reverse to liabilities. A payable should be recognised when the obligation has arisen and can be supported by approval, receipt, invoice, or another valid form of evidence.

Timing matters because premature recognition distorts the accounts, while delayed recognition hides the truth. Early invoicing may create a receivable that is not yet valid. Late recognition may understate a liability or hide revenue that should have been recognised. These errors matter most at month-end and year-end, when the records are used for reporting, budgeting, audit and decision-making. A balance recorded on the wrong date may be technically small but materially misleading if it affects the wrong period.

Where judgement is required, the judgement should be documented. The file should show what the basis of entitlement was, what evidence existed, who approved the recognition, what assumptions were made, and whether any uncertainties remained. If the balance depends on contract interpretation, the relevant clause should be retained in the file. If the amount was estimated, the estimation method should be visible. Control weakens quickly when judgement is undocumented, because future reviewers then have no way to assess whether the original treatment was reasonable.

4. End-to-End Receivable Lifecycle

The receivable lifecycle starts when entitlement arises and ends when the balance is settled, adjusted, provisioned or written off. The first stage is usually an operational trigger such as service completion, delivery, approval of a reimbursement claim, achievement of a milestone or release of a funded instalment. That trigger should be identified by the person or team responsible for the underlying activity, not invented later by finance. Finance can record the transaction, but the evidence should come from the operational source.

Once entitlement is established, an invoice should be prepared using the approved template and coding structure. It should identify the customer or counterparty, explain what is being billed, show the due date, state the currency and provide enough reference information for the recipient to understand the charge. Good invoices reduce dispute risk because they make the basis of the claim clear. Poor invoices increase delay because the counterparty has to ask basic questions before it can even review the amount.

Approval should happen before dispatch. In many organisations, the operational owner approves the content, finance approves the posting and a manager approves issue if the amount is material. Whatever the structure, the approval path must be recorded and traceable. The invoice should then be sent promptly and posted to the ledger with supporting evidence attached or linked. If dispatch is delayed, cash collection is delayed. If support is missing, dispute risk rises. If the reference information is incomplete, the invoice may be misapplied or ignored.

Collection does not happen automatically after issue. It needs planned follow-up. Reminder notices, calls, escalation and, where necessary, negotiation of instalments or settlement terms should form part of the process. Finance may drive the cycle, but the budget holder or operational owner often has the strongest leverage because that person understands the relationship and the underlying transaction. A strong collection process is therefore cross-functional rather than purely financial.

When cash is received, it should be matched quickly to the correct invoice or entitlement. Partial payments, overpayments, deductions and poorly referenced remittances should be investigated instead of being left unapplied. Unapplied receipts distort the ageing report and can make a debt appear older than it really is. If a receipt is reversed, disputed or later found to have been posted incorrectly, the record should be corrected immediately. The receivable lifecycle is only complete when the ledger, the bank and the supporting file agree.

If the item becomes overdue or disputed, it should move into a visible follow-up state rather than remaining in an undefined status. The file should show whether the balance is current, overdue, under review, disputed, provisioned, escalated, settled or written off. This matters because invisible items are the ones that quietly become losses.

5. Invoice Standards and Supporting Documents

Invoice quality determines collection quality. A valid invoice should include a unique sequential number, invoice date, due date, counterparty details, currency, description of the goods or services, amount due, tax or withholding treatment where relevant, payment instructions and a contact point for queries. It should also include the contract, order, award or other reference that connects the invoice to the original basis of entitlement. The clearer the invoice, the less room there is for avoidable dispute.

The supporting file is as important as the invoice itself. Depending on the transaction, the file may need a signed agreement, delivery note, acceptance certificate, service report, timesheet, partner acknowledgment, donor instruction, approval memo or another form of evidence. The reviewer should be able to see quickly what was delivered, why the amount is correct and why the counterparty is expected to pay. If those answers are not immediately visible, the file is weak even if the invoice itself looks professional.

Sequential numbering is a fundamental control. Invoice numbers should not be reused, skipped without explanation or manually overridden without authority. Missing numbers should be investigated because they may indicate a cancelled document, an untracked transaction or a control failure in the billing process. The invoice log should reconcile to the ledger so that every issued invoice can be traced from source to posting and then to collection, credit note or write-off. If a number cannot be explained, the control environment is incomplete.

Retention matters as much as issuance. Invoice files should be kept for the period required by policy, law, donor rules and contract terms. That usually means several years of accessible retention, with longer retention for regulated or externally funded transactions. Electronic storage should be secure, searchable and linked to the accounting record. If an invoice cannot be retrieved during review, then the organisation has lost part of its control evidence even if the posting was originally correct.

Templates are useful because they standardise the minimum information required. A good invoice template prompts the user to include the right references, the correct coding and the proper support. A poor template forces users to remember everything from scratch, which is where mistakes begin. The standard should make the right action the easiest action.

6. Credit Approval, Terms and Counterparty Setup

Before a counterparty is allowed to trade on account, the organisation should complete a reasonable onboarding review. That review may include identity verification, legal name confirmation, tax registration checks, bank account validation, sanctions screening where relevant, due diligence and credit assessment if exposure is material. The level of review should match the risk. A small recurring customer relationship does not require the same process as a high-value or high-risk relationship, but no counterparty should be created casually without validation.

Terms should be clear, documented and approved. Standard terms may be net 30 days, but actual terms can vary by contract, geography, donor rule, supplier agreement or statutory requirement. If the arrangement includes staged payments, retention amounts, milestone billing, early-payment discounts, late-payment interest or withholding conditions, those details should be recorded. Ambiguity in terms becomes ambiguity in collection, which creates overdue balances and disputes.

A credit limit register is useful where exposure is ongoing. The register should show the approved limit, the current outstanding amount, the reviewer, the approval date and the review date. This is valuable not only for customers but also for advances, inter-entity recoverables, partner balances and any other situation where the organisation is effectively extending credit. If the exposure exceeds the limit, the matter should be escalated. A limit that is routinely breached is not a control; it is only a note.

Counterparty setup is a control point because mistakes at this stage create later problems. If the legal name is wrong, payment may be delayed. If the tax code is wrong, the accounting may be wrong. If the bank details are wrong, the payment may go to the wrong account. If the reference structure is wrong, receipts may be misapplied. Good setup reduces future friction by preventing avoidable data problems at the start.

Controls over master data should include restricted access, independent review and periodic validation. A user should not be able to create or change a counterparty record without oversight. Bank detail changes should be especially protected because they are a frequent fraud risk. A disciplined onboarding process helps prevent both operational error and deliberate misuse.

7. Collections, Follow-Up and Dispute Handling

Collections should follow a planned rhythm rather than depending on memory. A typical cycle might include a polite reminder before the due date, a follow-up once the item becomes overdue, a formal demand after further delay and escalation if the amount remains unresolved. The exact timing can vary, but the principle should not: every step should be logged, visible and owned by someone specific. If reminders are not recorded, the organisation loses both control and evidence.

Every contact attempt should be documented. The log should show the date, the medium, the person contacted, the message sent, the response received and the next action. This is important not only for active collection but also for later write-off justification. If a balance becomes irrecoverable, the organisation should be able to show a reasonable and consistent attempt to recover it. A poor contact log weakens the case for provision, write-off and management oversight.

Disputes need their own handling because a disputed invoice is not the same as an ignored invoice. A counterparty may dispute an amount because of missing support, pricing disagreement, service quality issues, timing mismatches, contract interpretation, coding errors or entitlement questions. The dispute should be assigned to a named owner and a target resolution date. The exact reason should be recorded clearly, and the amount in dispute should be narrowed where possible. Vague disputes are harder to resolve than precise ones.

Possible outcomes include reissue of the invoice, credit note, partial settlement, revised timetable, additional evidence, or escalation to a manager, legal adviser, donor contact or procurement owner. The organisation should not allow disputes to drift while hoping they resolve themselves. Unresolved disputes are one of the most common reasons valid balances become old balances. A dispute that remains open too long damages both cash collection and reporting accuracy.

Collections work best when it is calm, factual and persistent. Aggressive behaviour may be appropriate in extreme cases, but most accounts respond better to clarity, documentation and consistent follow-up. The goal is not to win an argument. The goal is to convert a valid open balance into cash or a formally resolved exception.

Where external funding or contractual restrictions apply, no concession should be made casually. If a credit note or settlement is needed, the supporting rationale must be documented and approved. The organisation should protect the ledger without harming legitimate relationships or breaching its own rules.

8. Ageing Analysis, Monitoring and Month-End Reporting

Ageing analysis is the main management tool for seeing what is actually outstanding and how long it has remained open. A monthly aged debtor report should group items by age band and identify the counterparty, invoice number, due date, original amount, outstanding amount, currency, disputed status, owner, collection note and provision status. Without a disciplined ageing report, the organisation is managing by memory rather than by evidence.

The report should be reviewed every month by the preparer and at least one independent reviewer. Aged balances should be checked for reasonableness, and reconciling items should be explained. The control account must reconcile to the sub-ledger and the sub-ledger must reconcile to supporting records and bank receipts. If these do not match, the difference should be investigated and documented promptly. Unexplained reconciling items are often where deeper control failures hide.

Trends matter as much as absolute numbers. A rising number of 90+ day items, a growing concentration of balances with a few counterparties, or increasing days sales outstanding may indicate a weakening control environment. The report should therefore be accompanied by trend measures such as overdue percentage, collection rate, top debtor concentration, disputed balances and provision coverage. Management needs to know not only what is old, but whether the overall quality of receivables is improving or deteriorating.

Month-end close is where these controls meet. The close pack should include the aged schedule, account reconciliation, collection status, dispute log, provision calculation, write-off memo, credit note register and any relevant follow-up plan. If the close is rushed, unresolved items can drift into the next period and distort reporting. A disciplined close does not merely record transactions; it proves that the balance sheet reflects reviewed and supported amounts.

The same logic applies to payables. The accounts payable ageing should show what is due, what is overdue, what is in dispute and what has already been paid or reversed. Stale liabilities should be challenged, not simply left in place because nobody has time to investigate them. A good month-end process sees both sides of the ledger as active control areas, not passive posting buckets.

Recurring exceptions should trigger root-cause analysis. If the same item appears every month, the process rather than the balance is probably the problem. The close pack should therefore show not just the numbers but the management response to repeated issues.

9. Provisioning for Doubtful Debts

Provisioning recognises expected loss before a receivable is fully written off. It is a realism adjustment. The organisation may still be pursuing collection, but it no longer expects the full amount to be recovered. The provision reduces the carrying value of receivables to a more credible estimate of recoverable amount. Without provisioning, the balance sheet can look stronger than it really is.

There are several common methods. Specific identification is used when large or clearly troubled balances are reviewed individually. An ageing matrix applies different percentages to different age bands. An expected loss approach uses historical experience, counterparty risk, country risk, legal status, funding status or similar factors to estimate loss more precisely. In practice, many organisations combine these methods so that significant balances are assessed individually while the remainder are covered by a policy-driven ageing matrix.

Both quantitative and qualitative factors matter. Quantitative indicators include age, historical collection rate, size of the balance, number of broken promises, and the volume of overdue items. Qualitative indicators include the counterparty’s solvency, relationship status, contract enforceability, donor restrictions, project closure, operational instability and signs that collection attempts are being ignored or delayed. A current item may still be risky if the counterparty is weak; an old item may still be collectible if the dispute is valid and active.

The method should be approved, documented and applied consistently. If rates change, the reason should be recorded. If the provision increases, the movement should be explained. If the provision is released because a balance is collected or settled, the release should be visible in the movement schedule. A proper provision schedule shows opening balance, current charge, release, utilisation, write-off and closing balance. That schedule is one of the clearest indicators of whether management understands the quality of its receivables.

Provision policy should never be adjusted simply to improve the reported position or reduce effort. A weak provision creates false comfort. An over-strong provision can also be misleading if it is not grounded in evidence. The best provision policy is consistent, reviewable and based on actual risk patterns rather than on hope or pressure.

Ageing-based percentages should be supported by history where possible. If the organisation has high recovery on balances up to 60 days but poor recovery after 90 days, the policy should reflect that pattern. If certain counterparties or sectors are consistently weaker, that too should inform the provision. A good policy is evidence-based rather than purely formulaic.

10. Provision Calculation, Journal Entries and Write-Offs

The provision calculation should start with a clean ageing report. Each balance should be assigned the applicable percentage or specific estimate under the approved policy. The total provision is the sum of the ageing-based estimate plus any specifically identified amount. The schedule should show how each item was treated, why the chosen rate or assessment was used and who reviewed the result. A calculation that cannot be traced is not a reliable control.

Journal entries should reflect the accounting substance. A receivable is usually recognised by debiting debtors and crediting income or another entitlement account. Cash receipt debits bank and credits debtors. A provision debits bad debt expense and credits provision for doubtful debts. A write-off against an existing provision debits the provision and credits debtors. If the provision is insufficient, the excess loss is charged directly to bad debt expense. If there was no prior provision, the full write-off normally goes to expense.

Every journal should have a clear narration and support. A journal without explanation may technically balance, but it does not explain the business reason. The support should include the ageing schedule, provision worksheet, contact history, legal opinion if relevant, insolvency evidence, dispute log and any approval memo. The more significant the balance, the more detailed the support should be. A reviewer should not need to reconstruct the story from memory or from disconnected documents.

Write-offs require a higher standard than provisions because they remove the balance from the ledger. They should usually only be approved after reasonable collection attempts have failed or after evidence shows that further effort is not worthwhile. That evidence may include repeated reminders, calls, statements, legal review, insolvency confirmation, closure of the counterparty, donor or partner acknowledgment, or management assessment. The approval must follow delegated authority limits. A write-off is an accounting decision, but it is also a governance decision because it acknowledges that the asset is no longer expected to be realised.

Write-off timing matters. An item that should be written off too late can distort the balance sheet for months. An item written off too early can remove a balance that still had recovery potential. Timing should therefore be evidence-driven. The organisation should be able to explain why recovery was no longer reasonable at the time of approval.

A write-off memo should be readable on its own. It should explain the amount, the cause, the collection attempts, the evidence of irrecoverability, the financial impact and the approval route. If a memo cannot stand alone, then the control file is incomplete.

11. Credit Notes, Corrections and Counterparty Adjustments

Credit notes are used to reduce or reverse charges that are overstated, incorrect, returned, cancelled or otherwise no longer due in full. A credit note should reference the original invoice, use sequential numbering, be authorised by the appropriate person and be posted promptly. It is not enough to issue an informal correction by email or in a note outside the accounting system. The ledger itself must reflect the reduction in entitlement so that the debtor balance is not overstated.

Each credit note should be logged and reconciled to the original invoice. If the original invoice was already reported in management, donor or statutory submissions, the correction should flow through to those reports where necessary. If the correction is material, the review file should show why it arose, who approved it and what impact it had on the outstanding balance. A credit note without traceability can hide error or even abuse.

Not every credit note is the same. Some correct simple data errors such as incorrect quantity or price. Others reflect service failures, commercial settlements, rebates or contract variations. The accounting treatment should reflect the underlying reason. A correction of error is different from a negotiated concession. The documentation should make that distinction visible because it affects both reporting and governance.

The same discipline applies to reversal entries, duplicate invoices and misapplied receipts. Corrections should be timely, explained and linked to the underlying event. A poor correction process leaves stale items in the ledger and creates confusion for both collection and reporting. Good correction discipline improves ledger quality quickly because it removes known noise from the system.

Credit notes can also affect tax, donor reporting and project reporting. If the original invoice was linked to a tax treatment or funding code, the correction may need to be reflected in those records too. The correction should never be left half-processed across multiple systems. The accounting, the operational file and the reporting pack should all tell the same story.

Because of this, the approval route should always be clear. A minor correction may be approved locally, while a larger or more unusual adjustment may need higher-level sign-off. The file should always show who approved the adjustment and on what basis.

12. Inter-Office, Inter-Entity and Partner Recoverables

Internal balances between offices, branches, divisions or related entities often look simple but become difficult if they are not managed systematically. An internal recharge should identify the cost, the beneficiary, the allocation basis and the approving party. The receiving side should ideally acknowledge the charge, and the finance team should reconcile the internal balance regularly. If the receiving side disputes the amount or fails to acknowledge it, the balance should not simply be left open indefinitely.

Consistency matters. Internal recharges should be posted on a regular cycle and should use the same method for similar transactions. If one office posts monthly and another posts quarterly, the timing differences should be explained. If one entity uses a different allocation basis for the same cost, the internal balancing process becomes harder. The best method is usually the simplest one that can be applied consistently and supported by evidence.

Partner advances and sub-award recoverables need special care because they sit between finance, programme delivery and grant compliance. Funds advanced to partners should be tracked separately and supported by approved reports, acceptance evidence or liquidation documentation. If the partner has not substantiated the use of the funds, the balance remains a recoverable item and should continue to be aged and followed up. The ledger should always show how much was advanced, how much was justified and what remains outstanding.

Where partner balances are disputed, the issue is not just accounting. It may also involve programme delays, reporting gaps, contractual interpretation or missing documentation. Finance should work with operational staff rather than trying to clear the ledger in isolation. A healthy partner recoverable process therefore combines accounting discipline with contract follow-up and programme management. If the relationship is long-term, poor handling of a recoverable can create repeated problems in future periods.

Netting may be allowed in some internal situations, but it should not be used to hide unresolved items. A balance that offsets another balance still needs explanation. The records should show what was charged, why it was charged and how it was cleared. Netting should simplify settlement, not obscure accountability.

Regular reconciliation between internal entities is essential. Differences should be explained, not tolerated. The organisation should know whether an internal balance is genuine, disputed, settled, or merely pending processing.

13. Foreign Currency, Payables and Procure-to-Pay Controls

Receivables and payables in foreign currency should be recorded in the original currency and translated into reporting currency according to policy. The ledger should preserve both the original amount and the reporting equivalent. Exchange gains or losses arise when the rate changes between recognition and settlement, and those movements must be recognised properly. If the organisation has significant open foreign currency balances, period-end review becomes especially important because exposure can change quickly.

Foreign exchange is not only an accounting issue. It is also a treasury issue because timing can alter the amount finally collected or paid. Where exposure is material, treasury or central finance may need to advise on whether the balance should be settled sooner, offset internally, or monitored more closely. The accounting records should not hide the exposure. They should make the currency risk and the translation effect visible enough to support management decisions.

On the payable side, procure-to-pay should follow a standard route: requisition or approved request, purchase order or equivalent authorisation, goods receipt or service acceptance, supplier invoice, matching and then payment. The control objective is to ensure the organisation pays only for valid obligations that were ordered, received and approved. Two-way or three-way matching helps prevent duplicate, incorrect or unsupported payments. The more disciplined the process, the less likely it is that invalid liabilities will be paid or valid ones left unresolved.

Supplier master data is a sensitive control area. Bank details must be protected, validated and changed only with proper review. Duplicate invoice checks should be active. Access should be limited to the minimum required. If a supplier balance remains open for too long, the reason should be investigated. It may indicate an unmatched invoice, a missing receipt, a coding problem, a duplicate posting or a stale liability that should be reversed. Supplier statements should be requested and reconciled regularly, and unmatched items older than the agreed threshold should be escalated.

Credit balances with suppliers should not be ignored either. They may arise from overpayments, rebates, returns or duplicate settlements. Those balances should be tracked until they are refunded, offset or otherwise cleared. A positive supplier balance can be just as important as a debtor balance because it indicates money that may need recovery or future offset.

Unused supplier credits, stale liabilities and unresolved invoices all point to weaknesses in the procure-to-pay cycle. The organisation should be able to trace each open balance to a real cause and a real resolution path.

14. Tax, Statutory Balances, Segregation of Duties and Compliance

Tax and statutory balances often sit in special accounts and are therefore easy to overlook, but they deserve the same discipline as commercial receivables and payables. VAT, GST, withholding tax, payroll deductions, statutory levies, refund claims and similar balances must all be supported by valid documentation and reconciled to filings and returns. Where a refund is due, the claim should be tracked from submission through acknowledgment to receipt. Where a statutory amount is payable, due dates and filing references should be monitored so that penalties do not arise from simple delay.

The tax file should contain calculations, certificates, declarations, returns, correspondence and proof of submission or acknowledgment. If a tax receivable remains outstanding, it should be aged and followed up like any other debtor. If a statutory payable remains open, it should be visible with the relevant due date and filing reference. Tax balances are not optional items. They affect legal compliance and can create penalties or disputes if managed badly.

Segregation of duties is one of the most important controls in the entire process. Ideally, invoice creation, receipt posting, bank reconciliation, ageing review, provisioning, collection follow-up, credit note approval and write-off approval should be separated where practical. In a small office where full segregation is not possible, compensating controls should be used. These can include independent review, manager sign-off, surprise checks, documented approvals, periodic reconciliation by another person or oversight by a separate office.

Control failures often arise when the same person can create the balance, edit the support, approve the correction and reconcile the ledger without independent review. The organisation should avoid that structure wherever possible. Even if the team is small, there should still be meaningful checks. A strong control environment does not require a large staff, but it does require discipline, visibility and documented review.

Compliance also means aligning practices with delegated authority limits. Small write-offs may be approved locally, while larger balances require higher-level approval. Credit notes, payment plans, adjustments and exceptions should follow the same logic. The organisation should not invent approval shortcuts when a case becomes inconvenient. The authority structure exists precisely to prevent convenience from overriding control.

Training should make these rules practical rather than abstract. Staff should understand not just the policy, but the reason behind it. People follow controls better when they understand what the control protects.

15. Roles and Responsibilities

Several roles usually share responsibility for debtors, creditors and bad debts. Finance teams prepare invoices, post receipts, maintain ledgers, run ageing reports, calculate provisions and prepare write-off papers. Budget holders and operational managers confirm entitlement, support disputes, validate service delivery and help with collection escalation. Senior finance staff review unusual items, approve major adjustments and monitor concentration risk. Treasury or central finance may become involved in foreign currency exposure, liquidity issues, inter-entity balances or large recovery cases. Legal or compliance support may be needed for disputed, contractual or insolvency-related matters.

Every balance should have a visible owner. Ownership means someone is responsible for action, follow-up and escalation. Without ownership, items drift from month to month. The name of the owner should appear in follow-up notes, dispute logs or review schedules so there is no ambiguity about who is doing what. If an item remains unresolved, the owner should be able to explain why and what is being done next. Ownership does not mean the owner must solve everything alone. It means the item cannot float without accountability.

Responsibility should be matched with access to information. The person expected to resolve a dispute should have access to the contract, invoice history and correspondence. The person preparing the provision should have access to ageing data, collection evidence and counterparty status. Approvers should receive enough information to make a decision without having to assemble the file themselves. Good control design gives the right information to the right role at the right time.

Escalation routes should also be clear. An overdue item beyond the local threshold should move to a higher reviewer or manager. A disputed invoice that cannot be resolved operationally should go to someone with authority over settlement or interpretation. A material old item should not remain with the same preparer indefinitely. Escalation is not a sign that the process has failed. It is a sign that the process is moving through the right levels in an orderly way.

Role clarity is especially important in organisations with multiple offices or programmes. If everyone assumes someone else is following up, nothing happens. The organisation should therefore define who prepares, who reviews, who approves and who escalates each type of balance.

Good role design reduces both delay and duplication. It also makes audit evidence easier to produce because the reviewer can see who did what and when.

16. Good Practice Principles

Good practice starts with promptness. The sooner entitlement is recognised and invoiced, the sooner collection can begin. The sooner a supplier invoice is matched and approved, the sooner the liability can be settled correctly. The sooner a disputed balance is identified, the sooner it can be resolved. Delay is one of the main causes of aged items because every extra week gives the balance more time to drift, be forgotten or become harder to prove.

Consistency is the second principle. Similar transactions should be treated the same way from month to month unless a documented reason exists to change the treatment. The ageing methodology should not change casually. The provision policy should be applied consistently. Approval thresholds should be stable and visible. The organisation should not create one-off shortcuts every time a transaction becomes inconvenient. Consistency is what turns a process into a control.

Documentation is the third principle. A balance may be valid, but if the file is weak, the organisation will struggle to prove it. Every material debtor or creditor should have a clear evidence trail. That trail may include a contract, an order, delivery evidence, invoice, collection notes, reconciliation, approval memo and closure evidence. The stronger the file, the easier the review. When the file is weak, the organisation later wastes time trying to rebuild the story.

Practicality is the fourth principle. Controls must be strong enough to protect the ledger but simple enough for staff to use correctly every time. Overly complex processes get bypassed, while simple well-designed processes get followed. Templates, standard approvals, regular review cycles and clear responsibility usually produce better results than complicated but fragile procedures. Good practice is a balance of discipline and usability.

Practicality also means defining thresholds that matter. Not every minor item needs the same level of escalation, but every item should have a visible path. The system should help people do the right thing quickly rather than forcing them into workarounds.

Good practice is visible in routine behavior: invoices raised on time, reminders logged, reconciliations current, approvals traceable and exceptions reviewed. Those ordinary actions create the control environment that prevents major losses later.

17. Common Pitfalls

Common pitfalls are remarkably predictable. Late invoicing delays cash and weakens entitlement evidence. Poor coding creates reconciliation problems. Missing support makes it hard to prove why a balance exists. Inadequate ageing review allows old items to accumulate unnoticed. Weak provision policies hide expected losses. Delayed dispute resolution turns manageable differences into stale receivables. In each case, the problem is usually not that the organisation lacks information. The problem is that it has not forced action.

Another frequent problem is assuming that a balance will resolve itself because it has existed for a long time. Age does not equal collectability. A long-open balance may be recoverable, but it may also simply be neglected. The reverse mistake also happens: staff may assume an overdue item must be written off immediately when in fact it is still in active dispute or under formal settlement discussion. The answer is not automatic action in either direction. The answer is careful review supported by evidence.

A third pitfall is relying on memory instead of records. If only one person knows the history of an item, the control is fragile. The balance file should be good enough that another reviewer can understand it without asking the original preparer to narrate every step. Institutional memory should live in documents, logs and schedules, not just in one person’s head. When a team member leaves, weak documentation becomes a major operational risk.

Finally, organisations sometimes become accustomed to recurring exceptions. If the same debtor is always late, the same supplier statement never matches or the same reconciliation difference appears every month, the issue has become structural. Repeating exceptions should trigger root-cause analysis, not acceptance. When an item recurs, the process needs improvement rather than another temporary fix.

Another pitfall is overconfidence in system automation. Automation helps only when the underlying data and process are sound. If the setup is weak, the system may simply process errors faster.

Strong controls are usually simple to explain. Weak controls are usually justified by complexity. That pattern is often visible long before the numbers go wrong.

18. Audit Evidence and Review Expectations

Audit and internal review usually focus on whether the balance exists, is valid, is recoverable or payable and is recorded in the right period and at the right amount. For receivables, auditors often expect an aged debtor report, a reconciliation from the control account to the sub-ledger, sample support for invoices, evidence of collection follow-up, provision analysis and write-off approvals. For payables, they expect supplier reconciliations, invoice matching evidence, proof of receipt or service acceptance and confirmation that liabilities are complete and not duplicated. For bad debts, they want to see how management concluded that an item was doubtful or irrecoverable.

The key is not to prepare documents only when an audit is announced. The file should already exist in a coherent form. A clean month-end pack, indexed schedules and a traceable approval trail reduce audit friction because they answer basic questions before they are asked. Reviewers want to see the chain from source document to ledger to report to closure. If that chain is visible, the balance is much easier to defend.

Trend analysis is also important. If the provision rate falls while overdue debtors rise, that inconsistency needs explanation. If write-offs suddenly drop to zero, that may mean collection improved, but it may also mean management stopped recognising losses. If concentration in one customer or partner becomes high, the risk should be disclosed internally so it can be managed. Review is not only about single items; it is about whether the ledger is telling a stable, believable story over time.

Where findings arise, action should be documented. If an issue is deferred, the reason and next step should be recorded. If a balance is amended, the basis should be noted. If a disputed item is partly resolved, the remaining open amount should be stated clearly. Review packs should never rely on vague language such as “in progress” without saying who is doing what and by when. Transparency is the difference between a controlled exception and a hidden problem.

Evidence should also show that management actually looked at the report, not merely received it. Sign-off, minutes or review notes matter because they prove the report was used as a control tool.

Audit quality improves rapidly when the organisation can trace a balance from transaction to support to reconciliation to approval. That traceability is the core of the control environment.

19. Month-End Close Discipline

Month-end close is the checkpoint where debtor, creditor and bad debt controls are tested together. The organisation should run aged debtor and creditor schedules, reconcile control accounts, review disputes, check receipts and payments, assess provisions, prepare write-offs where appropriate and complete any necessary credit notes or reversals. The close pack should identify open items, owners and target dates. If these steps happen consistently each month, the ledger gradually becomes cleaner and more trustworthy.

Timing matters in close. The work should happen early enough that there is time to investigate unusual balances before the books are locked. If controls are left until the last minute, staff may approve entries without proper review simply to hit the deadline. That creates risk. A well-run close balances timeliness with accuracy by giving enough room for exceptions to be checked rather than rushed through.

Close discipline also means making sure items are not left unresolved just because they are old and familiar. Recurring reconciling items should be treated as control issues, not normal features of the month-end process. If an item appears every month, someone should own the root-cause analysis and the fix. Otherwise the organisation is merely postponing the same issue from one close to the next.

A good close pack should therefore show the story from opening to closing. It should explain what changed, what remained open, what was provisioned, what was written off and what action is still needed. The pack should be understandable to a reviewer who was not involved in preparing it. If it is only meaningful to the preparer, the close process is too person-dependent.

Close discipline is also about deadlines. Reconciliations should be completed on time. Follow-up should not be deferred until after reporting is finished. If the file is incomplete at close, the report is only partially trustworthy.

Good close discipline saves time later because it prevents unresolved balances from multiplying across periods. The best close is not just fast; it is complete, documented and stable.

20. Credit Notes and Error Correction

Credit notes are one of the most practical tools in debtor management because they allow the organisation to correct overbilling, return issues, missing service, pricing disputes or cancelled transactions. A credit note should refer to the original invoice and clearly identify whether it is correcting an error or reflecting a negotiated adjustment. It should be authorised and posted promptly so that the debtor ledger shows the true open amount. Delaying a credit note can make an overdue balance look collectible when it is not.

Every credit note should be traceable. The reviewer should be able to move from the credit note to the invoice, from the invoice to the contract or support and from the credit note to the approval. If the original invoice was reported externally or used in management reporting, the correction should flow through all related reports where relevant. Partial corrections should also be visible so the organisation does not lose track of what remains due and what was removed.

The reason behind the credit note matters. If it fixes a data error, the correction is mechanical. If it resolves a service dispute, the adjustment may require operational sign-off. If it reflects a concession or commercial settlement, it may need more senior approval. A well-written explanation helps reviewers distinguish normal corrections from significant exceptions. That distinction matters because a large number of casual corrections can hide a deeper process issue.

Other corrections follow the same logic. Duplicate invoices should be reversed. Misapplied receipts should be reallocated. Wrong customer or wrong project codes should be corrected. Incorrect balances should not be hidden in vague journals that nobody can trace later. Good correction practice is one of the easiest ways to improve ledger integrity because it prevents known errors from becoming long-lived balances.

Credit notes can also affect tax, donor reporting and project reporting. If the original invoice was linked to a tax treatment or funding code, the correction may need to be reflected in those records too. The correction should never be left half-processed across multiple systems. The accounting, the operational file and the reporting pack should all tell the same story.

The approval route should always be clear. A minor correction may be approved locally, while a larger or more unusual adjustment may need higher-level sign-off. The file should always show who approved the adjustment and on what basis. Without that, the correction itself becomes a control risk.

21. Internal Recoveries and Cross-Charges

Inter-office, inter-branch and inter-entity recoveries are common in multi-location organisations and can become surprisingly messy. A recharge should identify the cost, the beneficiary, the basis of allocation and the approval. The receiving unit should ideally acknowledge the charge, and the finance team should reconcile the internal balance regularly. If the receiving side disputes the amount or fails to acknowledge it, the balance should not simply be left open forever because the organisation is effectively arguing with itself.

Consistency and timing matter. Internal recharges should happen on a defined cycle and should use the same method for similar transactions. If one office charges monthly while another charges quarterly, the timing differences should be explained. If one entity uses one allocation basis and another uses a different basis for the same cost, the internal balancing process becomes difficult. A simple, documented method is usually better than a clever but opaque one.

Internal balances are not harmless just because both sides belong to the same organisation. If the balances are not matched, they can distort the asset and liability position, interfere with management reporting and create unnecessary reconciling items. Netting can be useful where permitted, but it should not be used to hide unresolved issues. The organisation should still know what was charged, why it was charged and how it was cleared.

Internal recoveries are also a good example of why finance and operations need to work together. Finance can calculate the numbers, but the operational owner often knows whether the cost was justified and whether the receiving side has a valid objection. A clean internal recovery process therefore depends on clear allocation rules, timely posting and active review rather than on finance acting alone.

Internal cross-charges should be easy to explain. If the file is unclear, the receiving side will resist, and the item will age. Clarity at the outset reduces disputes later.

Internal balances should be reconciled as aggressively as external ones. The fact that both sides belong to the same organisation does not make an unreconciled item acceptable.

22. Partner Advances and Sub-Award Recoveries

Funds advanced to partners or sub-recipients require special control because they often sit between receivables, programme delivery and grant compliance. The organisation should track how much was advanced, what the funds were meant for, what has been justified, what has been liquidated and what remains outstanding. If the partner has not submitted acceptable reports or evidence, the balance should remain visible as a recoverable item rather than being forgotten in a generic account.

Partner recoverables often need support from programme teams because finance may know the balance but not the delivery context. If the partner has delayed reporting, the issue may be an operational delay, a documentation gap or a compliance problem. The accounting should therefore be accompanied by a clear follow-up process: reminders, review of submitted reports, substantiation checks, offset against future instalments where allowed or recovery action if necessary. The ledger should reflect the status accurately throughout.

In donor-funded environments, the treatment can be especially sensitive because partner advances may be tied to specific budgets, reporting formats or eligibility rules. A recoverable amount may affect not just the balance sheet but also fund reporting and compliance status. The file should therefore show the contractual basis, the reporting requirement, the substantiation received and the reason any part remains open. This prevents later confusion over whether the amount was a programme advance, an expenditure advance or a true recoverable debt.

Where a partner balance becomes doubtful, the organisation must decide whether to keep pursuing recovery, agree a settlement plan, offset against future funding or treat the balance as a bad debt. That decision should be based on the facts and the agreement terms. A clear partner recoverable policy prevents ad hoc decisions and ensures similar cases are treated consistently.

Partner balances should not be left to drift because they belong to a trusted implementing relationship. Trust does not replace control. Even good partners need documentation, reconciliation and follow-up.

Programmes, finance and compliance all need to see the same status so that reporting, funding and operational decisions stay aligned.

23. Foreign Currency and Exchange Differences

Foreign currency balances require attention because exchange rates can change the amount ultimately collected or paid. A receivable or payable should be recorded in the transaction currency and translated into reporting currency in accordance with policy. Open balances should be retranslated at reporting date using the approved rate, and exchange gains or losses should be recognised. This ensures that the ledger reflects both the original claim and the current reporting value.

Foreign exchange movement should be understandable. Large gains or losses may arise simply from rate changes, but they may also indicate timing errors, missed settlements or incorrect postings. The finance team should therefore review exchange differences for reasonableness and ensure that the balance movement makes sense in context. If a foreign currency item remains open for a long time, the exposure should be monitored more closely because the eventual cash amount may differ materially from the original figure.

Treasury or central finance may need to advise when the exposure is material. In some cases, the organisation may wish to settle earlier, arrange internal offset or manage timing differently to reduce risk. The accounting records should not hide the exposure. They should make the currency, rate and retranslation movement visible enough for management to make informed decisions.

Foreign currency ageing is especially useful where balances sit open for a long period. A long-open foreign balance can be affected by both credit risk and exchange risk. That means the organisation may face not only collection uncertainty but also value uncertainty. The ledger should therefore show both the age and the currency exposure so the item can be reviewed properly.

Foreign exchange controls should include clear rate sources, consistent translation methods and review of unusual movements. If exchange gain or loss is unexpectedly high, the reason should be investigated. Timing, misposting or incorrect rate use are common explanations and should be checked first.

Foreign currency balances are often small individually but material in aggregate. That is why regular review matters more than occasional attention.

24. Supplier Balances and Procure-to-Pay Controls

Supplier balances are the payable side of the same discipline. The standard procure-to-pay flow should start with a requisition or approved request, move to a purchase order or equivalent authorisation, then goods receipt or service acceptance, then supplier invoice, then matching and finally payment. The aim is to ensure that the organisation pays only for valid, approved obligations. The more disciplined the matching process, the less likely it is that duplicate or unsupported liabilities will be paid.

Supplier master data is a major control point. Bank details must be protected, validated and changed only with proper review. Duplicate invoice checks should be active. Access should be restricted. The organisation should know who can create a supplier, who can change bank details, who can approve a payment and who can reconcile the ledger. If a payable remains open for too long, the reason should be investigated. It may be an unmatched invoice, a missing receipt, a coding problem, a duplicate posting or a stale balance that should be reviewed for reversal.

Supplier statements should be requested and reconciled regularly. Differences between the supplier statement and the accounts payable sub-ledger should be followed up promptly. Unmatched items older than an agreed threshold should not be ignored just because the counterparty has not complained. A liability that is no longer valid should be released only after proper evidence and approval. A liability that is still valid but unpaid should remain visible and controlled. Hidden supplier problems can become payment problems later.

Credit balances with suppliers also require attention because they may arise from overpayments, rebates, returns or duplicated settlements. Such balances should be tracked until they are refunded, offset or otherwise cleared. A positive balance in a supplier account can be just as important as a debtor balance because it indicates money that may need recovery or future offset. Good payable management therefore includes both liability settlement and credit balance control.

Procure-to-pay is one of the clearest control chains in finance because the documents should line up: request, order, receipt, invoice, payment. If they do not, the exception should be explained. Unexplained exceptions should not be treated as routine.

Supplier ageing and supplier statement reconciliation should be part of every monthly close. A stale payable should be challenged just as a stale receivable would be.

25. Tax and Statutory Balances
