Tax and statutory balances often require special handling because they may be subject to filing deadlines, legal obligations or recovery rules. The organisation should keep a clear record of VAT, GST, withholding tax, payroll deductions, statutory levies, refund claims and similar items. Each balance should be linked to the relevant return, certificate, submission or authority communication so that it can be traced easily. A tax receivable or payable that is not reconciled is a risk, not just an accounting item.

Refund claims should be tracked from submission to acknowledgment to receipt. If the authority queries the claim, the correspondence should be retained. If the claim remains outstanding, the ledger should still show its age and status. A statutory payable should similarly show its due date and filing reference so that management can see whether any deadlines are being approached or missed. Tax and statutory balances often have consequences beyond the ledger, so control must be at least as strong as for ordinary commercial balances.

Support files should include calculations, certificates, signed declarations, filed returns and evidence of submission or payment. In many cases a balance remains open not because it is invalid but because the external authority is slow. The organisation should be able to distinguish between “not yet settled” and “not valid”. That distinction matters because one requires patience and follow-up, while the other may require reversal or adjustment.

Multi-entity organisations may also need tax balances tracked by office, branch, fund or jurisdiction. The reporting should be structured so that compliance can be monitored at the correct level. A small missed filing in one entity may become a larger issue if the organisation cannot see it clearly. Tax control therefore requires both a detailed file and a clear summary view for management.

Statutory balances should not be treated as routine technical leftovers. They can become legal issues quickly if ignored. That is why timing, support and reconciliation matter so much.

Tax control is strongest when finance, compliance and operations all understand what has been filed, what is due and what remains outstanding.

26. Segregation of Duties and Access Control

Segregation of duties is essential because it prevents one person from creating a balance, disguising an issue and clearing it without oversight. Ideally, invoice creation, receipt posting, bank reconciliation, credit note approval, provisioning, write-off approval and journal posting should be split among different people or reviewed independently. In smaller teams where this is not possible, compensating controls such as independent checklists, manager sign-off and periodic sample review become even more important.

Access rights should reflect real responsibilities. A user who only prepares invoices should not be able to approve write-offs. A user who posts receipts should not also be able to edit supplier bank details. A person who can create a debtor should not be able to reverse the supporting evidence without review. These are not theoretical concerns. Weak access control is one of the easiest ways for errors to remain hidden and one of the easiest ways for fraud to occur.

Periodic access review matters because roles change over time. Staff move departments, leave the organisation or change responsibilities. If permissions are not updated, dormant or excessive access accumulates. The organisation should therefore review user rights regularly and remove any access that is no longer needed. That discipline is particularly important in the accounting systems that govern debtor and creditor balances because those systems are where control failures become financially visible.

Segregation and access control should extend beyond the accounting module when relevant. Billing, procurement, banking, master data, approval workflows and reporting interfaces all affect open balances. If someone can change data in one part of the chain without review, the entire control environment weakens. A robust system is one where no single person can create, conceal and clear a problematic balance without an independent check.

Controls should be tested, not just documented. If access rights are broad in practice, the written policy does not matter much. If approval workflows can be bypassed, segregation is only nominal.

Strong access control supports every other section of the ledger process. Weak access control weakens them all.

27. Delegated Authority and Approval Limits

Delegated authority limits define who may approve what. These limits should apply to credit notes, write-offs, payment terms, settlement discounts, disputed balance concessions, provisioning methodology changes and other exceptions. The organisation should not make ad hoc approval decisions because a case feels urgent. The authority framework exists so that urgent cases are still handled within a controlled structure.

Approval thresholds should be based on amount, risk and transaction type. A small routine write-off may be approved at local level. A large balance or unusual case may require senior finance approval or even executive review. The same idea applies to credit notes and concessions. If the value is significant or the circumstances are unusual, the approval should come from the appropriate authority level. This protects the organisation from arbitrary decisions and ensures that exceptions are visible to people with the right oversight.

Approval should not be treated as a formality. The approver should have enough information to understand the reason for the request, the amount involved, the support available and the risks. A signature without context is weak. A well-documented approval shows who approved, when, for what amount and on what basis. That evidence is important later if the item is audited or questioned.

Staff should also know the limits before they act. A control is stronger when people know in advance whether an item can be approved locally or must be escalated. Training, quick-reference guidance and simple threshold charts help prevent accidental breaches. The goal is not just to comply with authority rules after the fact. It is to make compliance the normal path of work.

Delegated authority should also cover exceptions to normal terms. If a debtor is given an extended payment plan, that should be approved at the right level. If a creditor settlement is accelerated or discounted, the approval should be visible. Exception handling is part of authority control, not separate from it.

Authority controls become meaningful only when the organisation actually follows them in routine cases, not just in major cases.

28. Practical Response to Problem Balances

When a balance becomes problematic, the response should be structured rather than emotional. The first step is to determine whether the balance is valid. The second is to determine whether it is collectible or payable in full. The third is to decide whether action is needed now or whether the issue can be monitored for a short time. That assessment should be evidence-based. Strong feelings are not a substitute for ledger review.

For a debtor, practical actions may include reissuing the invoice, sending a formal reminder, contacting the counterparty directly, escalating to the budget owner, requesting missing support, negotiating an instalment plan or moving the item to provision or write-off review. For a creditor, the practical actions may include obtaining missing documentation, confirming the obligation, reconciling the supplier statement or releasing an invalid liability with approval. For a partner recoverable, the response may include follow-up on outstanding reports, offset against future funding or a formal recovery plan.

Practical response also means documenting what has been done and what comes next. A problematic balance should not be left in the file with only a vague note such as “follow up later”. The file should show the action taken, the response received, the next step and the responsible owner. That record is useful not only for control but also for continuity if the original preparer changes role or leaves the organisation.

Some balances will not resolve quickly. That is normal. The aim is not instant closure but steady progress and clear visibility. A difficult item with a visible action plan is safer than an unresolved item sitting quietly with no owner. Good practical response therefore combines persistence, clarity and escalation rather than hoping the balance will disappear by itself.

Practical response should also distinguish between temporary delay and structural non-payment. The former may be managed by follow-up and patience; the latter may require provision, escalation or write-off. The file should show which one applies.

A clear action plan is often more valuable than a dramatic reaction. Control improves when the response is calm and documented.

29. Training, Templates and System Design

Training is essential because the process depends on many people understanding a few important rules. Staff need to know when a receivable can be recognised, what support is required, how to prepare an invoice, how to log a payment, when to escalate a dispute, how to age items, how to calculate provisions and how to complete a write-off request. If people do not understand the process, they will improvise. Improvisation in debtor and creditor control usually creates more work later.

Templates reduce variation and make the process easier to follow. Standard invoice templates, reminder letters, dispute logs, write-off schedules, provision worksheets, supplier reconciliation forms and approval memos all help staff capture the right information in the same way each time. A good template is not just a blank form. It prompts the user to provide the evidence and explanation needed for review. That makes the downstream control easier because reviewers do not have to chase missing basics.

System design is equally important. If the accounting system allows balances to be created without support, coded incorrectly or left unreconciled without visibility, the process will weaken over time. The system should encourage correct entry, link attachments, display ageing clearly and support approval workflows where possible. Automation can help with reminders, match checks and report generation, but only if the underlying data structure is sound. Automation built on weak data simply accelerates the same weakness.

The system should also surface exceptions. Disputed balances, long-open items, provisioned receivables, unresolved supplier credits and write-offs should be easy to see. Hidden exceptions are dangerous because they are forgotten. Visible exceptions are manageable because they can be reviewed and addressed. Good design therefore makes unusual balances stand out rather than burying them inside large account totals.

Training should be repeated when policies change or when staff rotate into new roles. A process that only exists in a manual but is not reinforced in practice will eventually drift.

Good design does not remove the need for judgement. It makes judgement easier to apply consistently and with less error.

30. Final Summary

Debtors, creditors and bad debts are not merely ledger categories. They are a complete control area covering entitlement, invoicing, approval, ageing, reconciliation, collection, payment, provisioning, write-off, correction, compliance and review. The organisation must know what is owed to it, what it owes others, what is still current, what is doubtful and what is no longer realistic to recover. Without that knowledge, cash flow, reporting and decision-making all become less reliable.

The principle is straightforward: recognise only genuine balances, support them with evidence, monitor them actively and remove or adjust them when the facts change. If a balance is collectible, collect it. If it is payable, settle it. If it is doubtful, provision it. If it is irrecoverable, write it off with authority. If it is disputed, assign ownership and resolve it. If it is stale, reconcile it. If it is foreign currency, retranslate it. If it is internal, do not ignore it. If it is statutory, keep it compliant.

The quality of this control area is often visible in the small things: whether invoices are complete, whether reminders are logged, whether reconciliations are current, whether provisions are reasoned, whether write-offs are approved, whether supplier balances are confirmed, whether disputes have owners and whether ageing reports are reviewed. When those small things are done well, the ledger becomes trustworthy. When they are done badly, the organisation eventually pays for the weakness through lost cash, bad reporting and avoidable audit findings.
Rows: 1
Table 18
Q&A Debtors, Creditors and Bad Debts
1. Q: What is the overall purpose of debtor, creditor, and bad debt control?
A: Show answer
It is a connected control area that keeps receivables, payables, losses, and settlement records aligned with reality. The purpose is to make sure the ledger does not just contain numbers, but also contains balances that are valid, current, supported, and actively monitored. A debtor balance should represent a real claim that still has value. A creditor balance should represent a real obligation that still needs settlement. A bad debt balance should represent a receivable that no longer has a realistic path to full recovery. When these three are controlled together, finance can see what is collectible, what is payable, what is disputed, what is overdue, and what needs escalation. This reduces the risk of overstated assets, hidden liabilities, weak collection follow-up, duplicate payments, or write-offs that are not properly approved. It also gives management a clearer picture of cash pressure, recovery risk, and the quality of open balances across the organization.
Send to the team:
- “Please review debtors, creditors, and bad debts as one monthly control area.”
- “Please make sure every open balance has an owner, an age, and a support file.”
- “Please explain any material item that is still open after normal settlement terms.”
Key point:
- These balances must be valid, current, and supported.
Official links:
- IRS Topic 453: Bad debt deduction
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
- FASB Receivables guidance
2. Q: What is a debtor, a creditor, and a bad debt?
A: Show answer
A debtor is a party that owes money to the organization, usually because the organization has already delivered goods, services, or funding support and is now waiting for settlement. A creditor is a party the organization owes, usually because it has received goods, services, or other obligations that are still unpaid. A bad debt is a debtor balance that no longer deserves full carrying value because recovery is no longer reasonably expected. These three concepts sit on the same settlement cycle. One side is what the organization is entitled to receive, one side is what it must still pay, and the third is what has become unrecoverable after reasonable follow-up. In practice, this means receivables and payables should never be mixed into one vague schedule. Each balance should be clearly identified so the team can decide whether it is collectible, payable, disputed, or stale. That distinction is important for reporting, cash planning, and control review.
Send to the team:
- “Please classify every open balance correctly before ageing or writing it off.”
- “Please separate receivable balances from payable balances in the control schedule.”
- “Please use one naming convention for debtors, creditors, and doubtful balances.”
Key point:
- A debtor is an asset claim, a creditor is a liability, and a bad debt is a loss risk.
Official links:
- IRS Topic 453: Bad debt deduction
- FASB Receivables guidance
3. Q: Why should debtor and creditor balances be monitored closely?
A: Show answer
Debtors often reveal weak invoicing, missing support, poor follow-up, or collection risk. Creditors often reveal duplicate payments, unpaid invoices, stale liabilities, or unresolved disputes. When these balances are not reviewed regularly, the ledger may appear complete while still containing errors, omissions, and aged items that distort reported performance. Monitoring is not only about finding old items. It is also about preventing old items from becoming normal. A balance that has been left open for too long may indicate a missing invoice, an unprocessed credit note, a settlement that was never matched, or a control gap in the approval chain. Close monitoring helps finance staff identify issues early enough to correct them before month-end or year-end reporting becomes difficult to defend. It also keeps the balance sheet meaningful, which matters for leadership, auditors, donors, and any other user who relies on the reported numbers.
Send to the team:
- “Please review open receivables and payables for validity, age, and support.”
- “Please explain any balance that has remained open beyond normal settlement terms.”
- “Please escalate recurring stale items instead of rolling them forward.”
Key point:
- Weak control usually shows up first in open balances.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2025)
4. Q: What is the difference between a normal debtor, a doubtful debtor, and a bad debt?
A: Show answer
A normal debtor is expected to be collected within the agreed terms and shows no major sign of recovery problems. A doubtful debtor has uncertain recovery because of delay, dispute, financial weakness, incomplete documentation, or poor response. A bad debt is judged irrecoverable after reasonable follow-up and review. The distinction matters because the organization should not treat every old balance the same way. Some balances only need routine collection action. Some need tighter monitoring and management review. Some need a provision because a loss is likely. Some need write-off because the chance of recovery has become too small to support keeping the full amount on the books. Good control depends on evidence, not just the passage of time. Age can warn you that a problem may exist, but age alone does not prove that a balance is doubtful or uncollectible. The classification should be supported by correspondence, collection history, dispute status, or other facts that show the true recovery position.
Send to the team:
- “Please move balances through normal, doubtful, and bad debt status based on evidence.”
- “Please do not use age alone as the only reason for write-off.”
- “Please document the reason when a balance changes status.”
Key point:
- Age alone is not enough; evidence must drive the classification.
Official links:
- IRS Topic 453: Bad debt deduction
- FASB Receivables guidance
5. Q: What is the difference between provisioning and write-off?
A: Show answer
A provision estimates expected loss while the receivable still remains on the books. A write-off removes the balance because recovery is no longer meaningfully expected. Provisioning is therefore a risk estimate and reporting judgment, while write-off is a removal action based on evidence that the asset is no longer recoverable in substance. A good policy keeps these two steps separate so management can see the full path from open balance to doubtful balance to final clearance. Provisioning is often used to reflect uncertainty before the final outcome is known. Write-off is used when the organization has concluded that the balance should no longer be carried as a recoverable asset. That means a receivable can be doubtful for some time before it is written off, and the process should allow for review, approval, and recovery attempts during that period. The purpose is not to hide loss. The purpose is to present a more realistic picture of what the organization can actually expect to collect.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep provisions and write-offs separate in both policy and posting.”
- “Please support write-offs with documented approval and recovery evidence.”
- “Please review provisions before deciding on write-off.”
Key point:
- Provisioning is an estimate; write-off is removal of the balance.
Official links:
- IRS Topic 453: Bad debt deduction
- FASB Receivables guidance
6. Q: Why is ageing analysis important?
A: Show answer
Ageing groups balances by how long they have been open and helps management see what is current, overdue, disputed, or likely to become a loss. It is a core control for both receivables and payables because it turns a long list of balances into a structured review tool. Ageing also helps identify process failures such as late invoicing, slow approvals, missing settlements, or weak creditor follow-up. A balance may still be collectible or payable, but ageing tells management where attention is needed first. A current balance is usually less risky than a balance that has remained open for many months. Ageing also supports better prioritization. Rather than treating all open items as equally important, the organization can focus on the largest, oldest, or most disputed items first. This makes monthly review meetings more useful and gives the finance team a consistent way to explain what has changed since the prior period.
Send to the team:
- “Please review ageing reports every month and follow up old items.”
- “Please explain long-outstanding balances individually, not in bulk.”
- “Please use ageing to prioritize action, not just to produce a report.”
Key point:
- Ageing is a signal, not proof, so it must be reviewed with other evidence.
Official links:
- FASB Receivables guidance
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
7. Q: When should a receivable be recognized?
A: Show answer
A receivable should be recognized only when the organization has earned the right to consideration or reimbursement and can support that right with evidence. Expectation alone is not enough. The ledger should reflect an actual enforceable or demonstrable claim, not a hopeful amount that has not yet been earned or approved. Recognition must also be timely so the organization does not delay recording valid claims and lose visibility over what is truly owed. This means entitlement should be checked before posting, and the amount should tie back to a contract, approved claim, completed service, accepted delivery, or another documented basis. If the organization records receivables too early, the balance sheet becomes overstated and collection follow-up becomes confused. If it records them too late, management may lose sight of real revenue or reimbursement that should already be visible in the books.
Send to the team:
- “Please confirm entitlement before posting any receivable.”
- “Please attach the basis of claim before recognising a debtor.”
- “Please do not post expected amounts without supporting evidence.”
Key point:
- Recognition follows earned entitlement, not optimism.
Official links:
- FASB Receivables guidance
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
8. Q: What evidence should support a receivable or payable entry?
A: Show answer
Supporting evidence may include contracts, purchase orders, delivery notes, service completion evidence, approvals, invoices, claims, donor instructions, or acceptance certificates. The file should show why the amount is valid and why settlement is due. Evidence should not only prove that a document exists, but also show the business basis for the amount, the timing of recognition, and the reviewer who approved it. A strong support file makes later reconciliation, audit review, and dispute handling much easier. For receivables, the evidence should prove that the organization earned the amount or has a right to bill it. For payables, the evidence should prove that the organization received what it ordered or accepted the obligation under the contract or policy. The support file should be organized so that a reviewer can move from source document to ledger entry without guessing. If the support is incomplete, the balance should be questioned, even if the ledger shows it as open.
Send to the team:
- “Please file the approval, support, and source document with every open balance.”
- “Please keep the claim basis visible in the schedule.”
- “Please make sure the support explains why the amount is due now.”
Key point:
- No evidence means weak control.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2025)
9. Q: What is the receivable lifecycle?
A: Show answer
The lifecycle starts with entitlement, continues through invoice preparation, approval, issue, posting, follow-up, collection, matching, and ends with settlement, correction, provision, or write-off. Each step has a control role, and failure at one step often creates problems later in ageing or reporting. If a receivable is posted without proper entitlement, the later collection process becomes harder to defend. If collection and clearing are not matched to the original invoice, the ledger can stay open even after cash has been received. The lifecycle should therefore be understood as a sequence, not as a single billing event. It begins before the invoice and ends only when the balance is cleared or formally resolved. That view helps finance staff see where the process may be breaking down. It also helps the organization assign responsibility to the right person at the right stage instead of waiting until the item becomes old and difficult to explain.
Send to the team:
- “Please track each receivable from entitlement to final settlement.”
- “Please match collections to the original invoice immediately.”
- “Please close the item only when ledger and bank agree.”
Key point:
- A receivable is not complete until the ledger and bank agree.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
- FASB Receivables guidance
10. Q: What makes a good invoice?
A: Show answer
A good invoice has a unique number, date, due date, counterparty details, amount, currency, description, references to the basis of entitlement, and payment instructions. It should make it easy for the recipient to review and pay. A well-designed invoice also reduces the risk of rejection, clarification requests, and delayed collection. For control purposes, the invoice should be traceable to the supporting contract or delivery record and should be easy to match against the ledger later. An unclear invoice can delay cash, create disputes, or force the recipient to ask for supporting evidence that should already have been visible. Good invoicing is therefore both an accounting task and a cash management task. It should help the customer understand what is being billed, why it is due, and how it should be settled. The easier the invoice is to verify, the faster the organization is likely to receive payment.
Send to the team:
- “Please use a standard invoice template with all required references.”
- “Please ensure the invoice ties back to the contract or service record.”
- “Please issue invoices promptly after entitlement is confirmed.”
Key point:
- Clear invoices reduce disputes and speed collection.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
11. Q: Why is sequential numbering important?
A: Show answer
Sequential numbering prevents reuse, supports completeness checks, and helps identify missing or cancelled documents. It is a basic control over billing and corrections. If numbers are missing, finance staff can investigate whether a document was lost, cancelled, reissued, or altered without approval. This makes the invoice trail more reliable and reduces the chance of hidden transactions. Sequential numbering also helps management check whether all expected invoices were actually raised and whether any documents were created outside the normal process. When the numbering sequence is controlled, it becomes much easier to confirm that nothing has been omitted. This matters for both receivables and payables because document completeness is one of the first areas reviewers examine. A weak numbering system may not by itself prove fraud, but it makes it harder to prove completeness. A strong numbering system reduces uncertainty and improves confidence in the records.
Send to the team:
- “Please reconcile invoice numbers to the invoice log and ledger.”
- “Please explain every missing or cancelled number.”
- “Please prevent duplicate numbering across the same series.”
Key point:
- Missing numbers must be explained.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
12. Q: How should a counterparty be set up before trading on account?
A: Show answer
The organization should validate identity, legal name, tax details, bank details, credit exposure, and any relevant due diligence before allowing credit. Master data should be protected because errors there create later payment and collection problems. A weak counterparty setup can cause invoices to go to the wrong party, payments to be misdirected, or balances to remain unresolved because the system does not correctly identify who owes what. Good setup is therefore both a compliance step and a practical control step. It helps the finance team avoid chasing the wrong entity, sending the wrong invoice, or paying the wrong supplier. It also gives the organization a reliable master record for future follow-up, correspondence, and ageing review. If the counterparty record is incomplete or inaccurate, the downstream reporting will be harder to trust even if the journal entries themselves are technically correct. Control starts before the first transaction, not after the account is already open.
Send to the team:
- “Please validate counterparty master data before any invoicing or payment.”
- “Please freeze or review any incorrect master record before posting.”
- “Please check bank and tax details before first use.”
Key point:
- Good setup prevents later collection and payment errors.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
13. Q: How should collections and disputes be handled?
A: Show answer
Collections should follow a planned reminder and escalation cycle, with every contact logged. Disputes should be assigned to an owner, narrowed where possible, and resolved through reissue, credit note, revised timing, or escalation. The key is to stop balances from remaining open simply because nobody is following them up. A dispute log should make it obvious who is responsible, what the issue is, what evidence is missing, and what the next action date is. Collections should not be left to memory or informal messages. A clear follow-up record helps the organization prove that it acted reasonably and consistently. Disputes should also be separated from genuine non-payment risk. Sometimes the amount is correct but the supporting document is missing. Sometimes the document is correct but the amount should be adjusted. Sometimes the debtor has not challenged the balance but simply stopped responding. The response should match the cause, not just the age of the item.
Send to the team:
- “Please document every follow-up and every dispute resolution step.”
- “Please assign one person to own each disputed balance.”
- “Please update the status of each item after every contact.”
Key point:
- Silent balances become old balances.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
14. Q: What should a month-end debtor and creditor report contain?
A: Show answer
It should show age band, counterparty, invoice or reference number, due date, original amount, open amount, disputed status, owner, collection notes, and provision status. The control account should reconcile to the subledger and supporting records. Management should be able to use the report not only to see balances, but also to understand what action is needed on each open item. A useful month-end report therefore combines ageing, explanation, and next-step responsibility in one place. It should also allow the reviewer to separate routine balances from exceptions. Items that are current and properly supported need less attention than items that are old, disputed, or repeatedly rolled forward. The report should give enough detail for follow-up without forcing the reader to search through multiple files. A strong month-end package turns raw balance data into an action list that can be reviewed, approved, and tracked in the next period.
Send to the team:
- “Please review ageing, reconciliation, and follow-up together at month-end.”
- “Please mark the next action and owner beside every material balance.”
- “Please explain any material difference between subledger and control account.”
Key point:
- Month-end reporting should prove the balance, not just list it.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
- FASB Receivables guidance
15. Q: How should doubtful debts be estimated?
A: Show answer
The estimate can use specific identification, ageing percentages, or expected loss methods based on history and risk indicators. The method should be approved, documented, and applied consistently. The estimate should reflect both current facts and reasonable forward-looking judgment, not simply management preference. A good allowance method should be reviewed against actual recoveries to see whether the assumptions still make sense. That review is important because an allowance is only useful if it reflects actual experience and current conditions. If the method is too optimistic, the receivables balance will be overstated. If it is too conservative, the organization may carry unnecessary expense. A reasonable estimation process should therefore be simple enough to apply every period, but detailed enough to capture real differences in risk. The team should also be able to explain why the selected method fits the organization’s customer mix, grant terms, payment history, or collection environment. The estimate should not appear arbitrary or disconnected from observed behavior.
Send to the team:
- “Please calculate provisions from an approved and traceable method.”
- “Please compare prior estimates to actual recoveries.”
- “Please document the assumption used for each age band or risk group.”
Key point:
- The provision should reflect actual risk, not convenience.
Official links:
- FASB Receivables guidance
- IRS Topic 453: Bad debt deduction
16. Q: What journal entries are usually used for receivables, provisions, and write-offs?
A: Show answer
A receivable is usually debited to debtors and credited to income or entitlement. Cash receipt debits bank and credits debtors. A provision debits bad debt expense and credits allowance. A write-off uses the allowance or posts directly to expense if no allowance exists. The journal should reflect the substance of the transaction and not just the convenience of the posting routine. In every case, the narration should make the reason for the entry easy to trace later. The entry should also connect clearly to the support schedule so that a reviewer can see what was posted, why it was posted, and which balance it affected. This matters because journal entries are not just accounting mechanics. They are part of the audit trail. If the narration is weak or generic, later review becomes harder. The accounting entry should therefore be written in a way that links the business event to the ledger impact without forcing the reviewer to guess the context.
Send to the team:
- “Please narrate every journal and attach the support schedule.”
- “Please ensure the posting matches the actual business event.”
- “Please use a standard journal format for provisions and write-offs.”
Key point:
- The journal must match the business substance.
Official links:
- FASB Receivables guidance
- IRS Topic 453: Bad debt deduction
17. Q: How should credit notes and corrections be handled?
A: Show answer
Credit notes should reference the original invoice, use sequential numbering, be authorized, and be posted promptly. Corrections should flow through the ledger and any linked reporting so the debtor balance is not overstated. If a correction is made outside the accounting system, the audit trail becomes weak and the true open amount may remain hidden in reports. This is why the source document, approval, and system posting must all line up. A correction should tell a clear story: what was wrong, why the correction was needed, who approved it, and how the books were updated. If the correction is partial, the remaining open balance should still be traceable back to the original charge. If the correction is full, the balance should be cleared in a way that can be explained later. This is especially important where disputes, rebates, billing errors, or revised service terms are involved. Delayed posting can make reports inaccurate even when the correction has already been agreed.
Send to the team:
- “Please post every correction through the accounting system, not by email only.”
- “Please retain the link between the credit note and the original invoice.”
- “Please clear the ledger at the same time the correction is approved.”
Key point:
- A correction must be traceable from source to ledger.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
18. Q: How should internal recharges, partner advances, and inter-entity balances be controlled?
A: Show answer
Internal balances should show the cost, beneficiary, allocation basis, and approval, and they should be reconciled regularly. Partner advances and sub-awards should stay aged and visible until justified or settled. The organization should not allow these amounts to sit in a generic account without explanation because that hides accountability and weakens donor or management reporting. A clear schedule should show what was advanced, what has been liquidated, what is still outstanding, and what evidence remains pending. This is important because internal and partner balances often become invisible when they are not reviewed on a dedicated schedule. The organization may then lose track of whether the amount is truly recoverable, still under review, or overdue for action. Control is stronger when the schedule identifies the original purpose, the responsible person, the reconciliation status, and the expected next step. That makes the balance easier to manage and easier to defend during audit or donor review.
Send to the team:
- “Please reconcile internal and partner balances on a regular cycle.”
- “Please keep partner advances in a dedicated control schedule.”
- “Please explain the allocation basis for every internal balance.”
Key point:
- Internal balances still need full accountability.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
19. Q: How should foreign currency balances be handled?
A: Show answer
The balance should be recorded in the original currency and translated into reporting currency under policy. Exchange gains or losses should be visible at settlement and period end. The key control point is to keep both the original transaction amount and the reporting-currency equivalent visible so that rate movements can be explained later. Long-open foreign currency items should also be reviewed for collectability and payment timing because exchange risk may rise as balances remain unsettled. This means the organization should not just monitor whether the customer or supplier will pay, but also whether the amount will move because of exchange rate changes. The original currency record helps explain the business event. The reporting currency record helps explain the financial statement impact. Both are needed. If a balance is left open for too long, the foreign exchange effect may become material even when the underlying business issue is small. That is why settlement timing and currency exposure should be reviewed together.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep both original currency and reporting currency visible in the file.”
- “Please review open foreign currency balances for rate movement and settlement timing.”
- “Please explain exchange differences separately from collection differences.”
Key point:
- Currency risk should not be hidden in the ledger.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
20. Q: How should payables and procure-to-pay controls work?
A: Show answer
The process should follow requisition, approval, purchase order or equivalent authorization, receipt or service acceptance, invoice matching, and payment. Supplier master data and duplicate invoice checks should be tightly controlled. This sequence ensures the organization pays only for obligations that were properly ordered, received, and approved. It also reduces the risk of overpayment, duplicate payment, and payment for items that never arrived or were never accepted. A proper control chain makes it possible to explain why the payable exists and why it was paid. It also helps stop payments that bypass policy or occur without evidence of receipt. The payable should not be treated as valid merely because an invoice was received. It should be valid only after the organization confirms the business event behind it. This protects cash and strengthens the integrity of the liabilities balance. Good procure-to-pay control also reduces tension between finance and operations because the approval path becomes visible to everyone involved.
Send to the team:
- “Please pay only for valid, ordered, received, and approved obligations.”
- “Please match the invoice to the receipt before payment.”
- “Please check supplier details and invoice duplication before posting.”
Key point:
- Matching is a key defense against invalid payments.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
21. Q: What should happen with tax and statutory balances?
A: Show answer
VAT, GST, withholding tax, payroll deductions, refund claims, and other statutory balances should be supported by filings, calculations, and acknowledgments. Open items should be aged and followed up like any other debtor or creditor. These balances are not simply accounting leftovers; they often represent compliance obligations with deadlines, penalties, and filing evidence attached. They should therefore be reconciled to returns, payment confirmations, and correspondence with the authority. A tax control schedule should make it easy to see what was filed, what was paid, what is still outstanding, and what proof exists. If the organization has a refund claim, the claim should be tracked separately from a normal receivable so the status is obvious. If there is an unpaid liability, it should be monitored with the same discipline as a supplier balance. The legal and compliance consequences make these items especially important to control carefully.
Send to the team:
- “Please reconcile statutory balances to returns and filing evidence.”
- “Please keep tax filing proof with the control schedule.”
- “Please track refund claims separately from trade receivables.”
Key point:
- Tax balances are compliance items, not routine leftovers.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
22. Q: Why is segregation of duties important?
A: Show answer
No single person should control every important step of a transaction where it can be avoided. Authorization, recording, custody, review, follow-up, and write-off approval should be separated or compensated with strong review. This reduces the chance that an error or misuse can be hidden from detection. It also makes the process easier to check because each person’s role is narrower and more visible. In practice, a strong segregation model means one person does not create the invoice, approve the adjustment, post the receipt, and clear the balance alone. If staffing is limited, compensating controls should be used, such as manager review, exception reports, or periodic independent checks. The goal is not bureaucracy. The goal is to make the process harder to abuse and easier to trust. Where segregation is not possible in full, the organization should be especially careful to document review and approval clearly so that the control chain remains visible.
Send to the team:
- “Please separate posting, approval, reconciliation, and write-off duties.”
- “Please add review controls where staffing is limited.”
- “Please avoid one person controlling the full cycle of a balance.”
Key point:
- Separation of duties reduces error and misuse risk.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2025)
23. Q: What are the main roles and responsibilities in debtor and creditor control?
A: Show answer
Finance prepares invoices, posts receipts, maintains ledgers, runs ageing, calculates provisions, and prepares write-off papers. Budget holders and operational managers support entitlement, disputes, and collection. Senior finance reviews unusual items and approves major adjustments. In a strong control environment, no one expects the finance team alone to solve every old balance without help from program, procurement, or management staff. Responsibility should therefore be shared, but clearly assigned. The people who created the business event often hold the best information about whether a balance is still valid or should be adjusted. Finance keeps the record and manages the control process, but other teams provide the operational explanation. That means responsibility should be documented in the process, not left to informal understanding. Clear ownership avoids blame-shifting, weak follow-up, and delays in clearing old items. It also helps management know who should respond when a balance becomes disputed or stale.
Send to the team:
- “Please assign one visible owner for every open balance.”
- “Please involve the budget holder in disputes that affect service delivery.”
- “Please make finance, operations, and management roles explicit.”
Key point:
- Ownership prevents balances from drifting month to month.
Official links:
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
24. Q: What is the main control objective across all of this?
A: Show answer
The main objective is to prove what the organization is owed, what it owes, what is collectible, what is payable, and what has already been settled. Every balance should be valid, supported, aged, and ready for review. That means the organization should be able to explain the origin of the balance, the reason it is still open, the action planned next, and the evidence used to support its current status. If any part of that chain is missing, the balance is not fully controlled. This is the central idea behind debtor, creditor, and bad debt management. The ledger should be a live record of settlement status, not a list of unresolved items that nobody has checked. Control should therefore connect source documents, accounting entries, follow-up action, approval, and final settlement. When those pieces line up, management can trust the open balance reports and use them for decision-making. When they do not, the reports lose value quickly.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep every open balance traceable from source document to final settlement.”
- “Please review validity, ageing, and settlement together.”
- “Please close or correct any balance that no longer matches the facts.”
Key point:
- If it cannot be traced, it is not controlled.
Official links:
- IRS Topic 453: Bad debt deduction
- GAO Financial Audit Manual, Volume 1 (2024)
- FASB Receivables guidance
25. Q: What are the main good practice principles for debtor and creditor management?
A: Show answer
The main principles are prompt action, consistency, documentation, and practicality. In practice, this means invoicing quickly, matching and approving supplier bills on time, reviewing disputes early, applying the same ageing and provision rules each month, and keeping controls simple enough for staff to follow correctly. Good practice is not about adding more forms; it is about making the existing process reliable, repeatable, and easy to verify. A practical control is one that people actually use every month without skipping steps. If the process is too complicated, staff may bypass it or apply it inconsistently. If it is too loose, it may fail to identify risk. The best approach is to design a process that is clear, documented, and easy to review, while still strong enough to protect the organization. The team should understand what to do, when to do it, and what evidence must be kept. That is what makes the control process sustainable.
Send to the team:
- “Please act early on invoices, disputes, reconciliations, and approvals.”
- “Please apply the same review standard every month.”
- “Please keep the process simple enough that it is actually followed.”
Key point:
- Good control is prompt, consistent, documented, and usable.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 557
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
26. Q: What are the most common pitfalls in debtor and creditor control?
A: Show answer
Common pitfalls include late invoicing, poor coding, missing support, weak ageing review, slow dispute resolution, weak provision policies, and recurring exceptions that are treated as normal. Another major risk is assuming a balance will resolve itself just because it has been open for a long time. In practice, old balances often survive because no one owns them, the source file is incomplete, or the system allows a temporary item to remain outstanding indefinitely. Those weaknesses eventually appear as reporting errors, audit findings, or cash-flow surprises. A balance may look harmless when it is first posted, but if the process never forces a review, it can remain open for months or years and create a false picture of performance. Repeating exceptions are especially important because they usually point to a root-cause problem rather than a one-off mistake. The organization should therefore look for patterns, not just balances. It should ask why the problem keeps appearing and what control failed to stop it earlier.
Send to the team:
- “Please review recurring and stale balances as control issues, not routine items.”
- “Please investigate the root cause, not just the balance amount.”
- “Please track repeat exceptions separately.”
Key point:
- Repeating exceptions usually signal a process weakness.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 557
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
27. Q: What audit evidence is usually expected for receivables and payables?
A: Show answer
Reviewers usually expect aged schedules, control account reconciliations, source documents, collection follow-up, supplier statements, invoice matching evidence, proof of receipt or service acceptance, provision analysis, and write-off approvals. The file should show the chain from source document to ledger to report. If the evidence chain is incomplete, auditors may question whether the balance exists, whether the amount is accurate, or whether management has reviewed the balance properly. The support file should therefore be built around the actual control process, not just around the ledger printout. Good evidence does more than confirm that an item was recorded. It proves why it was recorded, who reviewed it, whether it remains open, and what the expected next step is. It also helps auditors understand whether a balance is still valid or should have been adjusted earlier. The better the support file, the less time the review process usually takes and the easier it is to defend the reported figure.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep a clean support file for every material debtor and creditor.”
- “Please make sure the file proves existence, accuracy, and review.”
- “Please retain both the source document and the reconciliation evidence.”
Key point:
- Audit evidence should prove existence, validity, and timing.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
- IRS Publication 557
28. Q: Why is month-end close discipline important for open balances?
A: Show answer
Month-end close is the checkpoint where debtor, creditor, and bad debt controls are tested together. A disciplined close keeps ageing current, reconciliations complete, disputes visible, and provisions or write-offs reviewed before reporting is finalised. Without close discipline, open balances can roll forward for months without proper challenge, and old exceptions can become part of the normal ledger. A strong close makes the reporting cycle shorter, cleaner, and easier to explain to leadership or auditors. It also creates a habit of timely review, which is important because stale items are much harder to explain later. Month-end close should not be treated as a purely technical posting exercise. It should be a control process that checks whether the ledger still reflects the real position as of the reporting date. If balances are not reviewed before closing, the organization may keep reporting numbers that are technically posted but practically outdated.
Send to the team:
- “Please complete ageing, reconciliations, and exception review before the books are locked.”
- “Please clear or explain material items before the reporting sign-off.”
- “Please use close review to catch stale items early.”
Key point:
- A strong close makes the ledger cleaner and more reliable.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 557
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
29. Q: How should credit notes and error corrections be handled?
A: Show answer
Credit notes should clearly refer to the original invoice and show whether they correct an error, settle a dispute, or reflect a negotiated adjustment. Corrections should be authorised, posted promptly, and traced through the ledger and related reports so the true open amount is visible. If the credit note exists but the ledger is not updated, the report remains wrong even though the correction was approved. That is why timing, documentation, and posting must be treated as one chain of control. The organization should be able to see what changed, why it changed, and how the open balance now looks. This matters because a correction that is only discussed verbally can be forgotten later or applied inconsistently. The correction process should therefore leave a visible trail in the accounting records, the support file, and the ageing schedule. If the issue is partially corrected, the remaining balance should still be understandable. If it is fully corrected, the account should be cleared in a way that is easy to verify later.
Send to the team:
- “Please link every credit note to the original invoice and approval basis.”
- “Please update the ledger and subledger at the same time.”
- “Please avoid off-system corrections without a matching journal.”
Key point:
- Corrections should be traceable, authorised, and timely.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
- IRS Publication 557
30. Q: What are internal recoveries and cross-charges in multi-entity NGOs?
A: Show answer
Internal recoveries are charges between offices, branches, or entities for shared costs. They should identify the cost, the beneficiary, the allocation basis, and the approval, then be reconciled regularly so internal balances do not age unnoticed. This matters because cross-charges can look like ordinary receivables or payables if the documentation is weak. A clear explanation is needed so people can tell whether the balance is a genuine external claim or simply an internal settlement awaiting clearing. These items can create confusion when one entity recognizes the charge but the other has not yet cleared it. They can also distort funding or cost reports if the charge is not allocated consistently. A dedicated schedule should therefore show the original amount, the reason for the recharge, the settlement status, and the remaining difference if any. Internal balances are not less important just because they are inside the same group. They still require ownership, review, and regular reconciliation.
Send to the team:
- “Please post internal charges on a fixed cycle and reconcile them every month.”
- “Please explain the allocation basis for every internal balance.”
- “Please keep inter-entity items out of generic suspense accounts.”
Key point:
- Internal balances still require control and reconciliation.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
- IRS tax-exempt organization terms
31. Q: How should partner advances and sub-award recoveries be controlled?
A: Show answer
Advances to partners or sub-recipients should be tracked by amount, purpose, justification received, liquidation status, and outstanding balance. If reports or evidence are missing, the amount should remain visible as recoverable rather than being forgotten in a generic account. The organization should use one schedule that shows what was advanced, what has been spent, what has been cleared, and what still needs support. That makes it easier to see whether the balance is simply pending or whether it has become overdue or unsupported. These items can create serious control issues if the partner spends funds but delays liquidation, or if the organization fails to follow up when support is missing. A good schedule helps the finance team and program team work from the same facts. It also supports donor reporting because it shows whether the advance remains justified under the grant or partnership terms. The aim is not just recovery. The aim is accountability for the use of funds.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep partner advances and liquidations in a separate reconciliation schedule.”
- “Please escalate unsupported partner balances early.”
- “Please show the remaining balance, not just the total advance.”
Key point:
- Partner balances need clear substantiation and follow-up.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 557
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
32. Q: How should foreign currency balances be handled?
A: Show answer
Foreign currency receivables and payables should be recorded in the transaction currency, translated at the approved rate, and retranslated at the reporting date. Exchange gains and losses should be recognised, and long-open balances should be reviewed for both credit risk and exchange risk. This is important because the amount the organization expects to collect or pay in local terms can move as exchange rates move. The ledger should therefore show both the original currency exposure and the reporting currency effect clearly enough to explain later adjustments. A balance that is still valid in business terms can still change in reported value because of currency movement. That is why the organization should not wait until settlement to think about the rate effect. It should track rate changes as part of the monthly review. The team should also distinguish between collection issues and exchange issues so that the cause of movement is not misunderstood in the report.
Send to the team:
- “Please review open foreign currency balances for rate movement and settlement timing.”
- “Please keep the original currency and reporting currency in the schedule.”
- “Please separate exchange gain or loss from cash collection issues.”
Key point:
- Foreign currency balances need regular retranslation and review.
Official links:
- FASB ASU 2016-14
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
33. Q: What are the key controls in supplier balances and procure-to-pay?
A: Show answer
The standard flow is requisition, approval, purchase order, goods receipt or service acceptance, supplier invoice, matching, and payment. Strong controls also include supplier master data protection, duplicate invoice checks, restricted access, and regular supplier statement reconciliation. This sequence protects the organization from paying for goods not received, paying twice, or paying an amount that does not match the agreed terms. It also helps support invoice validity when supplier queries or disputes arise later. A supplier balance should be explainable through the source documents and not just through the ledger. The process should also make it easy to identify goods received but not invoiced, invoices received but not yet matched, and invoices disputed because of quantity, price, or quality issues. Strong payables control is not only about stopping bad payments. It is also about making sure the liabilities balance is complete and accurate. The more the process matches the real business cycle, the more reliable the payables report becomes.
Send to the team:
- “Please do not pay supplier balances until the request, receipt, and invoice agree.”
- “Please reconcile supplier statements to the payable ledger.”
- “Please investigate all unmatched or duplicate supplier items.”
Key point:
- Payables should be supported by a complete document chain.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
- IRS Publication 557
34. Q: How should tax and statutory balances be controlled?
A: Show answer
Tax and statutory balances should be tied to the relevant return, certificate, filing, or authority communication. Refund claims, payroll deductions, withholding, and levies should be tracked by status, due date, and support so that compliance risk is visible. They should not be left as unexplained ledger items because that can hide late filing, underpayment, or missing credit claims. A good control schedule should show the liability or claim, the return period, the submission date, the payment date, and the outstanding status. These items are especially important because delays can create penalty exposure or make it harder to prove compliance later. A clear control file should let the reviewer see whether the amount has been filed, paid, claimed, or still disputed. If a balance is on the books but no related return can be found, the organization should treat it as a control issue and investigate immediately. Statutory items are not casual accruals. They require proof and careful reconciliation.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep all filed returns, calculations, and submission proof in one control file.”
- “Please reconcile each statutory balance to its filing and payment evidence.”
- “Please escalate any unmatched tax item immediately.”
Key point:
- Statutory balances are compliance items, not just accounting items.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 557
- IRS tax-exempt organization terms
- GAO Green Book
35. Q: Why are segregation of duties and access control important?
A: Show answer
Segregation of duties prevents one person from creating, hiding, and clearing a balance without oversight. Access rights should match real job responsibilities, and periodic reviews should remove permissions that are no longer needed. This reduces error and fraud risk across debtor, creditor, and journal processes. It also makes problems easier to investigate because the system trail shows who could do what, who actually did it, and who reviewed it. Access control is not only about preventing misuse. It is also about limiting the impact of a simple mistake. If too many permissions are given to one person, that person may accidentally create more problems than the review process can catch. The organization should therefore align access with role, monitor changes in access, and review user rights regularly. This is especially important where the same staff member is involved in posting, reconciliation, and reporting. Proper access control supports better accountability and better audit trails.
Send to the team:
- “Please separate invoice creation, posting, approval, and review wherever possible.”
- “Please review user access and remove outdated permissions.”
- “Please limit system rights to what each role truly needs.”
Key point:
- No single person should control every key step.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
- IRS Publication 557
36. Q: What is delegated authority and why does it matter for open balances?
A: Show answer
Delegated authority defines who may approve write-offs, credit notes, concessions, settlements, and other exceptions. Approval limits should be based on amount, risk, and transaction type so significant cases are escalated properly and decisions remain controlled and visible. Without clear authority limits, staff may approve items they should not, or delay action because they are uncertain who may sign. The result is either unauthorized action or avoidable bottlenecks. Delegated authority matters because open balances often require decisions, not just posting. A collection compromise, a settlement discount, or a write-off is not a routine clerical step. It is a judgment decision that should be made at the right level. The approval chain should be visible in the file so that the organization can show who authorised the action and why the action was acceptable under policy. That clarity protects the organization and reduces confusion for reviewers.
Send to the team:
- “Please check the approval limit before authorising any exception.”
- “Please escalate amounts above the delegated threshold.”
- “Please document the approver and rationale for each exception.”
Key point:
- Approval limits make exception handling controlled and visible.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
- IRS Publication 557
37. Q: What is the practical response to a problem balance?
A: Show answer
The response should be structured: confirm whether the balance is valid, assess whether it is collectible or payable in full, decide whether it needs immediate action, and document the next step and owner. Actions may include reminders, reconciliation, settlement plans, provisions, or write-offs. The main purpose is to move the item from confusion to a decision point. Even if the final answer is not immediate, the organization should at least know what evidence is missing and who is responsible for obtaining it. A problem balance should never be left with no owner and no next date. The team should decide whether the item is waiting for a document, waiting for a reply, waiting for approval, waiting for cash, or waiting for a formal adjustment. That level of clarity helps ensure the item does not simply roll into the next period unchanged. The problem may still be unresolved, but it should not be untracked.
Send to the team:
- “Please assign an owner and next action for every disputed or stale balance.”
- “Please document the decision path before changing the ledger.”
- “Please set a next review date for each unresolved item.”
Key point:
- Problem balances need evidence-based action, not assumption.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 557
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
38. Q: Why are training, templates, and system design important in this control area?
A: Show answer
Staff need clear guidance on invoicing, reminders, dispute logs, provisions, and write-offs. Templates reduce variation, and the system should support attachments, ageing, approvals, and exception visibility so the process is easier to use correctly. Automation helps only when the underlying data structure is sound. If the process is weak, a faster system just creates faster errors. Training and templates therefore make the control area more repeatable and less dependent on memory or individual style. A useful template tells staff exactly what fields are required and what evidence must be attached. A good system makes it easy to keep those fields current and reviewable. Training should explain not only what to do, but why the step matters. That way the process becomes part of everyday practice instead of an occasional compliance exercise. The result is better accuracy, better review, and less rework later in the cycle.
Send to the team:
- “Please use standard forms and report layouts for every balance review.”
- “Please train staff on the process before automating it further.”
- “Please make sure the system supports attachments and approval history.”
Key point:
- Good process design makes control easier to follow.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
- IRS Publication 557
39. Q: How should management review and escalation work for open balances?
A: Show answer
Management review should focus on old items, large items, disputed items, and balances that recur each month without resolution. Escalation should follow an agreed path so unresolved balances are moved from preparer to reviewer to manager and then to an approving authority when needed. This ensures the issue does not disappear into the next reporting cycle. A useful review meeting asks what changed, what is still missing, what action is due next, and who owns the follow-up. The goal is not simply to discuss balances, but to force decisions or next steps. Items should either be cleared, supported, provisioned, written off, or formally deferred with a reason and a next review date. Review should therefore be active and practical. It should not just be a meeting where numbers are read aloud. The review process should create a record of action, ownership, and progress so the next month begins from a controlled position rather than from the same unresolved starting point.
Send to the team:
- “Please review large, old, and disputed items first.”
- “Please escalate unresolved balances using the approved chain.”
- “Please record the decision and follow-up date for every material item.”
Key point:
- Review should lead to action, not just discussion.
Official links:
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
- IRS Publication 557
40. Q: What is the final message of the debtors, creditors, and bad debts framework?
A: Show answer
Debtors, creditors, and bad debts are a full control area covering entitlement, invoicing, ageing, reconciliation, collection, payment, provisioning, write-off, correction, compliance, and review. The organization must know what is owed, what it owes, what is current, what is doubtful, and what is no longer realistic to recover. The discipline is not only about accounting entries; it is about proving the balances are real, current, supported, and actively managed. When the process works well, the ledger becomes a reliable record of settlement status rather than a pile of unresolved items. The best outcome is not merely a clean report. It is a system where each open balance can be explained, followed up, and cleared in a controlled way. That means the team needs good source documents, good approval discipline, good review habits, good ageing analysis, and a clear route for escalation. If those pieces exist, the control area becomes predictable and useful. If they do not, the ledger becomes harder to trust with every passing month.
Send to the team:
- “Please keep every balance supported, reviewed, and cleared or corrected when the facts change.”
- “Please use one monthly control file for debtors, creditors, and bad debts.”
- “Please do not let open items roll forward without explanation.”
Key point:
- Control depends on prompt action, evidence, and regular review.
Official links:
- IRS Publication 557
- IRS tax-exempt organization terms
- GAO Green Book
- GAO Standards for Internal Control
Rows: 40
Table 19
Notes
Bank Accounts and Banking Controls

1. Overview

Bank accounts are one of the most important control points in any organization because they sit at the intersection of cash, accountability, and operational continuity. Nearly every meaningful financial flow eventually reaches a bank account in some form. Salaries move through payroll accounts, supplier invoices are settled from operating accounts, customer receipts are deposited into collection accounts, tax obligations are paid from designated accounts, treasury teams monitor balances to assess liquidity, and management relies on banking data to understand whether the organization can meet short-term commitments. Because so much depends on bank accounts, even a small weakness in control can spread into accounting errors, payment delays, reputational damage, compliance issues, and fraud exposure. A bank account is never merely an administrative convenience. It is a governed asset that requires discipline, evidence, and oversight.

Strong banking controls matter for reasons that go beyond theft prevention. They also prevent duplicate payments, unauthorized signatory changes, missing statements, stale reconciliations, dormant accounts that remain open, and weak audit trails that make the organization unable to explain what happened to its own cash. A weakness may first appear as something minor, such as a missing approval, an unsigned form, or a transfer that was not reviewed in time. But these small failures often reveal a larger pattern of weak discipline. Banking controls usually deteriorate gradually rather than suddenly, which is why organizations often fail to notice problems until losses have already accumulated. The danger is not only the event itself, but the habit of tolerating exceptions until the exceptions become normal.

A sound banking framework therefore treats each account as part of a complete life cycle rather than as a one-time administrative record. An account should be opened for a clear purpose, approved at the correct level, entered into a central register, protected by access restrictions, monitored through statements and reconciliations, and formally closed when it is no longer needed. The control chain must remain intact from opening to closure. If any stage is missing, the whole system becomes less reliable. Strong banking control makes unauthorized activity harder to begin, easier to detect, and much harder to conceal over time. It also creates clarity for honest staff, because people know what is expected, what evidence is required, and where responsibility sits.

2. Scope and Coverage

The banking control environment must cover every account that the organization owns, operates, funds, or uses in practice. That includes operating accounts, payroll accounts, savings accounts, foreign currency accounts, receipt accounts, project accounts, merchant settlement accounts, escrow-like arrangements, and temporary or special-purpose accounts opened for branches, subsidiaries, field offices, or controlled entities. If the organization benefits from an account or is financially affected by it, then the account belongs inside the control framework. Excluding a small, temporary, or rarely used account is a common mistake because those are often the accounts that escape review for the longest period. Weak control rarely begins with the busiest account. It more often hides in the account that everyone assumes is too minor to matter.

Scope should also extend to bank products and services that move money or create liability exposure. These include overdrafts, credit lines, letters of credit, guarantees, direct debit authorities, sweep arrangements, cash management portals, payment gateways, mobile banking applications, and card settlement facilities. They may not look like ordinary deposit accounts, but they can still move cash, create obligations, or alter available balances. A narrow framework that focuses only on one main checking account creates a false sense of safety. Risk can be building elsewhere in the banking relationship, especially where different platforms, currencies, or business units are involved. The control question should not be limited to “Which account did we use today?” It should also ask, “What banking exposure exists anywhere in the organization’s footprint?”

The scope must also include the full transaction chain around each account. Cash deposits, transfers, card receipts, cheques, automatic debits, inter-account sweeps, foreign exchange conversions, bank fee deductions, and chargebacks all need to be recorded and reviewed. Weakness often appears in less visible flows because staff assume those transactions are routine, low-value, or too small to matter. A complete framework removes that assumption and recognizes that even infrequent or low-value transactions can create major control failures if they are not monitored properly. Coverage should be broad enough to include all money movement, but focused enough that each account still receives practical attention. If coverage becomes too vague, the framework looks complete on paper while leaving real gaps in operation.

3. Core Principles and Control Objectives

Effective banking controls rest on a small number of enduring principles. No bank account should exist without documented approval and formal inclusion in the account register. No single person should be able to create, authorize, release, and conceal the same transaction without independent review. Every payment should be supported by source documents and checked against an approved authority limit. Every balance should be reconciled regularly to the accounting record, and every discrepancy should be explained and followed up. All important banking records should be retained in a complete, accessible, and retrievable file. These principles remain valid whether the bank is physical or digital, local or international, simple or advanced. Technology changes the method, not the underlying need for accountability.

Those principles support several practical objectives. They protect funds from unauthorized movement. They improve the accuracy of accounting records. They create a defensible audit trail for management and external reviewers. They help detect suspicious activity early enough to stop it before losses grow. They support treasury planning by making balances and exposures visible. A strong control system does more than record what happened. It creates conditions in which hidden activity becomes harder to sustain. The organization should be able to answer clear questions: where the money came from, where it went, who approved it, and why the transaction was permitted in the first place. If those answers are not immediate and evidence-based, the system is not adequately controlled.

The best framework is usually the one that people can actually follow. A simple process that is performed every month is more valuable than a sophisticated policy that staff ignore under pressure. Control quality is measured by consistency in practice, not elegance on paper. If employees understand what must be done, when it must be done, who must approve it, and what evidence is required, the organization is far more likely to maintain discipline. Clarity, repetition, and accountability are more important than complexity for its own sake. A control that everyone understands but sometimes executes is better than a perfect control that nobody can realistically sustain. The goal is dependable behavior, not decorative policy.

4. Governance and Accountability

Banking control requires explicit accountability at multiple levels. Senior management is responsible for approving the banking policy, setting risk appetite, and ensuring the framework is actually implemented. Finance leadership usually owns the day-to-day governance of bank accounts, including registers, reconciliations, statement review, and coordination with banks. Treasury may manage liquidity, funding, and foreign currency exposure. Operational managers may retain authority to initiate transactions within approved limits. The key point is that responsibility must be clear, assigned, and understood. If no one is clearly responsible, problems remain unresolved because everyone assumes someone else will handle them. A bank account without ownership is a control gap waiting to be exploited or simply forgotten.

Every account should have a named owner or responsible unit. Every reconciliation should have a preparer and an independent reviewer. Every signatory should be identified and formally approved.
